coredata.dc:description: The use of various biomass derivatives and wastes to produce carbon dots (CDs) represents a green synthesis approach that holds great potential for commercialization by offering economically viable, abundant, and sustainable resources. The process is reliable and reproducible by producing high-quality CDs with controllable size and surface structure, high photoluminescence (PL), and narrow emission. This review highlights the potential of biomass wastes and derivatives as a valuable resource for the production of CDs, showcasing their economic viability and environmental sustainability. The discussion is focusing on optimizing the green synthesis of CDs from different biomass precursors, including small molecules (e.g., sugars, polymers, amino acids, proteins, etc.) and biomass wastes (e.g., leaves, stem, root, flower, pollen, etc.), elucidating the underlying synthetic mechanisms, and exploring various modification techniques. The pursuit of high-quality CDs materials is also realized through innovative modification strategies, such as surface passivation, precise morphology and size control, and heteroatom doping, which enable tailoring the properties of CDs according to specific requirements while minimizing waste generation. The formation mechanism and tuning of optical properties were also discussed in detail, focusing on the comparison with CDs using other precursors. We further introduced the sustainable applications of these green CDs on renewable energies, such as photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, supercapacitors, lithium-ion batteries, and photovoltaics. Finally, this review provides a perspective on the challenges, potential advancements and future directions in the field of the green CDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The use of various biomass derivatives and wastes to produce carbon dots (CDs) represents a green synthesis approach that holds great potential for commercialization by offering economically viable, abundant, and sustainable resources. The process is reliable and reproducible by producing high-quality CDs with controllable size and surface structure, high photoluminescence (PL), and narrow emission. This review highlights the potential of biomass wastes and derivatives as a valuable resource for the production of CDs, showcasing their economic viability and environmental sustainability. The discussion is focusing on optimizing the green synthesis of CDs from different biomass precursors, including small molecules (e.g., sugars, polymers, amino acids, proteins, etc.) and biomass wastes (e.g., leaves, stem, root, flower, pollen, etc.), elucidating the underlying synthetic mechanisms, and exploring various modification techniques. The pursuit of high-quality CDs materials is also realized through innovative modification strategies, such as surface passivation, precise morphology and size control, and heteroatom doping, which enable tailoring the properties of CDs according to specific requirements while minimizing waste generation. The formation mechanism and tuning of optical properties were also discussed in detail, focusing on the comparison with CDs using other precursors. We further introduced the sustainable applications of these green CDs on renewable energies, such as photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, supercapacitors, lithium-ion batteries, and photovoltaics. Finally, this review provides a perspective on the challenges, potential advancements and future directions in the field of the green CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: New materials and techniques have been developed to address global challenges related to energy, environment, and health. These advances aim to conserve energy, promote a sustainable ecosystem, and enhance human well-being [1]. The development of carbon nanomaterials has the potential to greatly benefit science and society. However, while these nanomaterials can be produced with minimal energy consumption and without the use of toxic chemicals or fossil fuel-based resources, they also have the potential to violate green chemistry principles [2]. To fully unlock the benefits of carbon nanomaterials, it is essential to prioritize green synthesis methods and utilize renewable resources.

There has been significant interest in carbon materials like graphite, graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and fullerenes due to their potential applications [3]. In 2004, Scrivens et al. introduced a breakthrough by electrophoretically separating CNTs, yielding a fluorescent fraction. This finding shattered the conventional notion that carbon materials are always dark and black [4]. Since then, researchers have adopted a top-down approach to create various emissive carbon dots (CDs) using sources like soot [5], CNTs [6,7] and graphite [8]. CDs fall under the category of zero-dimensional (0D) carbon materials, with lateral sizes <10 nm and lattice planes resembling those of graphite, i.e., (100) and (002) lattice spacings [9,10]. To increase their hydrophilicity and unique chemical properties, CDs typically possess hydroxyl, carboxylic, carbonylic, and epoxy functional groups along their edges. They primarily consist of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and hydrogen (H) [11]. The term ‘carbon dots’ has evolved due to diverse synthetic methods and raw materials, encompassing carbon nanodots (CNDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). CDs offer several attributes such as low cost, biocompatibility, facile preparation, photostability, hydrophilicity, and ease of functionalization [12,13]. Their exceptional electrochemical, biological, and optical properties make CDs preferable in various research domains, including energy devices [14], bioimaging [7,15], optoelectronics [16,17], sensing [18], catalysis [19], and more. The initial focus was on studying the photoluminescence (PL) properties of CDs, which were discovered and extensively investigated [5,20,21].

Various methods have emerged for preparation of CDs since Sun et al. demonstrated their production through graphite ablation in 2006. These methods encompass both top-down and bottom-up techniques [6,22,23]. In the top-down approach, materials like graphene, CNTs, carbon fiber, activated carbon, and carbon black are broken down into smaller components. Techniques like chemical oxidation, arc-discharge, ultrasonic agitation, electronic scissoring, hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment, and laser ablation are employed [12,24]. While top-down processes are straightforward, easily manipulated, and yield relatively high quantities, they entail significant expenses, demanding specialized equipment, ample raw materials, and harsh conditions that limit their adaptability. Bottom-up methods, on the other hand, involve controlled interactions of organic molecules with sp2 carbon domains, which makes CDs synthesis from relatively inexpensive organic molecules, adding to their interest. These techniques encompass template interactions, microwave irradiation, pyrolysis, and hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment. The success of materials applications is typically determined by their chemical and physical characteristics, including chemical functionality and morphology. The synthesis of materials plays a crucial role in this process. Recently, green chemistry principles have been used to enhance sustainability. Biomass-based resources are commonly employed as green carbon sources, and greener synthesis approaches have been implemented to achieve this goal.

Inexpensive and readily available saccharides, organic acids, and biomass wastes can serve as precursors, especially when aiming for sustainability and favorable quantum yields (QYs), notably with biomass waste as the starting material. A scalable production process for CDs can be achieved by utilizing high‑carbon-content biomass with a dense distribution of heteroatoms. This not only contributes to sustainable green energy strategies but also offers an eco-friendly solution for solid waste disposal [25,26]. Although there is plenty of waste biomass available at little to no cost, it is often overlooked as a viable alternative raw material in agriculture, forests, food, and animal wastes. By utilizing these wastes, CDs can be produced without the use of chemicals, resulting in a renewable and cost-effective end product that can be scaled up for commercial production. Over the past few decades, there have been significant advancements in the synthesis, characterization, and applications of CDs. Utilizing green precursors for CDs synthesis offers numerous advantages, such as their environmental sustainability, renewability, accessibility, affordability, high yield, and abundance of carbon sources. Various waste materials, including grains [27], seeds [28], milk [29], tea [30], coffee [31], fruits [32], and leaves [33] have been used to produce CDs. Additionally, readily available food sources such as nescafe [34], and caramel [35] have also been utilized for this purpose.

Using biomass can help achieve a bioeconomy, which is a model of economic growth based on renewable biological resources [36]. While it is a significant advancement to shift from conventional precursors to biomass-based carbon sources, the durability of products can still be compromised by high temperatures or long reaction times. The diverse range of natural biomass allows for the production of CDs with variable structures and properties, highlighting the potential for innovation in this field. The CDs derived from biomass waste have immense potential in various applications, including heavy metal ions detection [37], biomedical applications [38], energy storage devices [39], water purification [40], environmental research [41], and more [42]. Recently, to meet the growing demand for clean hydrogen energy with zero emissions, CDs have also offered significant advantages in various sustainable applications including photocatalysis, electrocatalysis and photovoltaics, owning to their high electric potential, large surface area, and uniform distribution in solution. Additionally, CDs enhance the conductivity and reduce agglomeration in the composite catalysts, whereas the size and surface effects of CDs can reduce the dissociation energy of intermediates, preventing agglomeration and corrosion and thereby boosting electrocatalytic kinetics [43,44]. The pursuing of high efficiency and selectivity in these sustainable applications inputs new challenges on the preparation and manipulation of these biomass-derived CDs beyond their advantage of low cost.

Recently, there have been several published reviews on CDs, which have focused on their chemical and surface functional properties, optical sensing, and biological applications [45–47]. This review is aimed to provide a comprehensive overview of the current state of CDs that have been prepared using environmentally friendly green synthesis methods. This review stands out among other published reviews due to its comprehensive focus on multiple contributions. These include identifying natural biomass feedstock that can be utilized as a source of CDs, devising advanced synthesis methods, and successfully producing CDs from biomass-derived feedstocks. The review begins with a brief introduction to these green synthesis methods, followed by a discussion on various modifications and functionalization strategies used to incorporate CDs into different materials. Furthermore, the review also explores the various sustainable applications of CDs. Finally, the review concludes by providing a summary of the existing challenges and future directions, drawing on the progress made in ongoing research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Classification, structure, and properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The advancements in synthesis techniques and the plentiful supply for precursor materials (such as natural substances, polymers, small organic molecules, graphite, etc.), a wide range of CDs have been developed. Consequently, several types of CDs have been mentioned in the literature, namely GQDs, carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs). [48] GCDs consist of multiple layers of graphene, with typical lateral dimensions in the tens of nanometers range. These GCDs contain various defects, oxygenated functionalities, conjugated sp2 π domains, and electron traps, all of which negatively impact their conductivity and PL. The shape of GCDs, whether hexagonal, elliptical, spherical, or triangular, depends on the precursor and synthesis conditions used. The preparation of GCDs typically involves a top-down approach using different carbon derivatives like CNTs, graphene oxide, graphite, carbon fibers, and soot. Both the CDs and CNDs nanoparticles generally have a spherical or nearly spherical shape. Some researchers consider both CNDs as CDs, particularly if their size is less than 10 nm. Another group of CDs, known as CPDs, are polymeric nanoclusters formed through chemical cross-linking or aggregation. Condensation reactions typically result in non-conjugated cross-linked networks. Prolonged reaction time at higher temperatures leads to increased carbonization and stronger π − π* interaction between atoms. In this review, for simplicity, all types of carbon dots will be collectively referred to as CDs, without further classification.

Generally, CDs consist of a carbon core with a graphite lattice, either amorphous or semi-crystalline, and an organic surface with plenty of functional groups with a particle size below 10 nm (Fig. 1 ) [47,49]. The cores of CDs are often nanocrystalline or amorphous, exhibiting sp2 hybridization [50]. In some studies, sp3-hybridized carbon atoms form diamond-like structures as well [51]. Surface functional groups can be identified using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) or Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). During the synthesis process, groups such as CO, CO, and C–OH usually end up on the surface of CDs. Furthermore, specific functional groups can be introduced on the surface of CDs by selecting the appropriate carbon or dopant precursors during synthesis.

CDs possess characteristics of both semiconductors (carbon core) and organic molecules (surface). The covalent binding of surface functional groups to the carbon core greatly influence the physical and chemical properties of CDs [52–54]. Scientists have been captivated by the array of exciting properties found in all types of CDs. These properties include excellent water solubility [12], limited toxicity [55]. the potential for efficient functionalization, strong PL, [56] reliable photochemical and physicochemical stability [57,58], good conductivity [59,60], and biocompatibility [6]. CDs also exhibit an extended absorption range that extends into the UV–visible and near-infrared regions, with specific absorption characteristics depending on their surface functionalities, composition, shape, and p-domains. In the realm of clinical research, CDs offer numerous benefits such as low toxicity and high biocompatibility [61,62]. Moreover, the structure of CDs facilitates chemical modifications using carboxyl, carbonyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups. Such modifications enhance sensitivity, selectivity, and optical characteristics by expanding the range of excitation and emission wavelengths [63].

CDs possess various optical properties, including optical adsorption and PL across different spectral ranges, electrochemical luminescence, and chemiluminescence. An important optical property specific to CDs is the up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL), which occurs through a two-photon absorption phenomenon. UCPL is particularly advantageous for cell bioimaging and biosensing applications, making CDs ideal for drug delivery, bioimaging, and biosensing [64]. Additionally, CDs serve as catalysts and storage systems for energy [24]. Similarly, the CDs can be easily and rapidly synthesized and functionalized. To enhance their optical, chemical, and physical properties, the surface of CDs can be modified and functionalized, enabling the creation of more sensitive biosensors [65,66]. Furthermore, CDs have a significant role as photocatalysts in water treatment and other environmental applications [67–70]. Their π-π interactions, fluorescence emission, and biocompatibility contribute to CDs being effective bioimaging agents in detection platforms [68]. Moreover, CDs offer potential in wound healing due to their antibacterial activities [67].

The carbon core may consist of a carbon ‘skeleton’ with various structural characteristics, while the surface functional groups exhibit complex molecular behaviors and have a profound impact on their physical and chemical properties. The properties of CDs are jointly determined by the carbon-like structure of the core and the organic-like structure of the surface. The integration of characteristics seen in carbon material and organic small molecules grants CDs significant flexibility in regulating their structure and physical and chemical properties, which present new challenges [71]. Along this line, the preparation process of CDs derived from small organic molecules or biomass is complex, and controlling their structure and properties poses additional challenges, placing limitations on their broader applications. Moreover, the formation process of CDs is highly complicated, and the detailed chemical intermediates and structures of the final products remain unclear.

CDs are expected to be useful in energy applications because of their good electrical conductivity and structural diversity. As an energy storage medium, CDs have several advantages. First, their large surface area compatibility with active electrode materials makes them stable and easily adsorbable. Second, combining CDs with active electrode materials create multiple electrochemical sites due to their rich surface functional groups and diverse structures. The specific capacity, cycle stability, and performance of CDs can be improved by bonding them with electrode-active materials like metal oxides and conducting polymers. Third, doping CDs with heteroatoms enhances their electrical conductivity and electrocatalysis by providing more active sites. By adjusting the phase structures and morphology of the composite electrode, the coordination between CDs and metal ions can further enhance device performance. Moreover, CDs have also found applications in separators and electrolytes. By carefully utilizing their unique structure and properties, CDs can contribute to more efficient and advanced energy storage methods.

Anastas and Warner [72] established the twelve principles for chemical synthesis in the Pollution Prevention Act of 1990. Their aim was to design products and processes that reduce contamination at its source, following the highest level of the pollution-prevention hierarchy. Natural carbon precursors are preferred for producing CDs based on Green Chemistry principles due to their low cost, environmental friendliness, and increased availability [73,74]. The bottom-up method, which utilizes harsh chemicals but results in minimal waste, is used to determine the morphological parameters of CDs under different reaction conditions and concentrations. However, this approach has limitations such as its unfavorable environmental stability, lack of understanding of modeling and mechanisms, potential bioaccumulation or toxicity, requirements for complex devices, difficulty in recycling, and the need for complex analysis. To address these challenges, the green synthesis method is employed, enhancing environmental sustainability by regulating, controlling, cleaning, and remediating the process. Green synthesis involves limiting or avoiding toxic chemicals, as well as reducing long reaction times, high temperatures, pressures, and lengthy procedures [75]. Techniques such as hydrothermal synthesis, microwave irradiation, ultrasonication, ozone oxidation, and self-exothermic synthesis are employed in the green CD synthesis process. The “bottom-up” approach, while less environmentally friendly, is simpler and more cost-effective, typically producing CDs with well-defined sizes and shapes. The particle size and surface state of CDs significantly impact their PL behavior and QY. The diversity of CDs is primarily influenced by the carbon precursors and synthesis employed [76,77]. Various synthesis parameters affect, including temperature, reaction media, types of dopant, carbon precursors, and the ratio of carbon source to reaction medium, affect the quality and optical properties of CDs [78].

Due to their solvent-free processes, ease of operation, scalability, and utilization of highly abundant and cheap biomass sources, hydrothermal and microwave methods are most commonly used for high-value biomass utilization. The moisture content of raw materials does not limit the use of these methods. In hydrothermal reactions, a batch reactor is employed to withstand the high temperatures necessary for carbonization while containing all vapor emissions to increase the reaction pressure. This leads to more efficient digestion of organic materials and allows for longer heating periods without volume loss through evaporation [79]. Hydrothermal CDs exhibit high PL and QY. Their synthesis involves the use of non-toxic renewable precursors and solvents, ensuring chemical stability and non-toxicity in the synthesized CDs. Despite being an energy-intensive process, a low-energy synthesis method should be prioritized. Additionally, the safety profile of the synthesized CDs should be considered, as the production of several intermediates and side products is possible. The emphasis on green CD syntheses principles will guide the discussion of previously reported in following subsections.

The conventional top-down and bottom-up CDs synthesizing approaches have presented economic and environmental challenges. These methods require the use of toxic solvents, expensive precursors, hazardous organic molecules, and high energy input. As a result, there has been a significant focus on developing a more sustainable method for synthesizing CDs by using less harmful precursors. Researchers have explored various synthetic and natural carbon-containing substances to achieve this goal [80]. One promising approach is to utilize bioresources as carbon precursors for CDs synthesis. There are several techniques available for synthesizing green CDs using bioresources, including hydrothermal carbonization, microwaves, chemical oxidation, thermolysis, etc. The following section provides brief descriptions of these methods for preparing CDs using bioresources.

The sustainable and efficient production of CDs require the selection of green resources, such as sustainable synthesis methodologies. A comparison between biomass and harmful chemicals like aromatic hydrocarbons reveals that biomass is a more cost-effective and safer option. There are currently three types of biomass available: protein products, organisms, and waste products. Embracing the concept of sustainable future, CDs are made from recyclable and renewable materials. The synthesis of CDs involves utilizing a greener synthesis methodology, incorporating self-exothermic, base catalysis, reduction methods, and other sustainable manufacturing techniques. These processes can be carried out at optimal temperature without the need for external energy or specialized equipment. In biomass production, biomass raw materials act as reaction precursors, requiring external energy and high-temperature sources. The versatile materials available for CDs synthesis allow for the creation of CDs with varying structures and characteristics. For industrial applications and large-scale production of CDs, it is crucial that raw materials are reusable, enabling cost-effective synthesis. Compared to chemical-based synthetic approaches, the preparation of CDs through biomass requires lower reaction temperature (100–200 °C). The choice of solvent in CDs production is determined by the dissolving capacity of the raw materials. Organic components of biomass typically react with organic solvents, while materials from biomass react with water. Utilizing the “bottom-up” technique, biomass-derived CDs can be produced as organic species polymerize and can be calcined at high temperatures. Alternatively, a “top-down” synthesis approach can convert or degrade waste carbon sources into nano-sized CDs. CDs can be prepared using various precursors, and the properties of the resulting materials can be influenced by their core structure and functional groups. Examples of some green precursors and their functional groups are provided below.

A variety of biomass-derived small molecules, such as glucose and fructose, are used as precursors for synthesizing CDs. These molecules contain -OH and -CHO groups, which can be used to surface functionalize the CDs. Similarly, various polymers like polyethylene glycol (PEG) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) are used as precursors in CDs synthesis. PEG, containing -OH groups, enhances biocompatibility and solubility of CDs, while PEI provides NH2 groups for their surface functionalization. The most common methods of synthesizing CDs involve using amino acids, where CDs can be surface functionalized using -NH2 and -COOH groups present in amino acids like glycine [81]. These modifications enhance the stability and water solubility of the CDs. Additionally, proteins, such as albumin and lysozyme, have been successfully used in the synthesis of CDs. These proteins contain -COOH and -NH2 groups that complement the surface functionalization of CDs.

CDs are typically derived from nitrogen and carbon sources, such as milk and other protein-rich foods [82–84], leading to a lower QY compared to CDs obtained from other biomass sources. It is believed that CDs derived from biomass sources exhibit a higher QY due to the presence of functionalities containing nitrogen atoms. These functionalities create a new atomic state, known as the N-state, which enhances radioactive recombination and suppresses non-radioactive recombination by trapping electrons [85]. Additionally, amino functionalities in nitrogen-doped CDs can act as electron donors, increasing their conjugation ability and indirectly enhancing the QY [86]. The production of CDs involves various small molecules, including phenolic compounds, cellulose, and glucose. Through polymerization methods and carbonization at high temperatures, substances containing numerous carbons around small molecules can be converted into polymers, which are then used to create luminous CDs. To further enhance the fluorescence of CDs, their surfaces can be passivated by introducing heteroatoms such as phosphorus, sulfur, and nitrogen. The structure and optical properties of CDs are influenced by the materials used to create them, with CDs made from biomass known to have higher QY due to the presence of nitrogen atoms in organisms. Biomass feedstock requirements for CD are typically natural and can include pulp, juice, seeds, and other components. CDs derived from different parts of organism can exhibit diverse qualities, with those containing nitrogen atoms showing improved QY.

Various types of biomass precursors can be used to manufacture CDs, including plant leaves, fruit peels, animal waste (such as feathers, fur, bones, and hairs), as well as paper and plastic wastes. These materials typically have a high carbon concentration, making them suitable for CDs production. Additionally, CD precursors have been discovered in eggshells, fish scales, coffee grounds, and other biomass sources that exclusively contain proteins, lipids, and carbohydrates. These biomaterials can be converted to a range of CDs with diverse biomedical and environmental applications, leading to their biocompatibility and antibacterial activities [87]. CDs can also be directly synthesized by using fruits [88], vegetables [89], beverages, juices [90], and organic waste [91]. A variety of chemical or physical processes can transform these precursors into CDs that are predominantly composed of carbohydrates and other organic compounds. For example, banana peels have been used to synthesize CDs by breaking down carbohydrates into small carbon-based molecule during the synthesis process [92].

Similar processes have been used for synthesis of CDs using rice husks [93], potato starch [94], and other agricultural waste [95]. In addition to breaking down of large molecules to small carbon-based molecules, CDs can be synthesized through hydrothermal or pyrolysis conversion of cellulose and other polysaccharides-rich precursors [96]. The resulting CDs exhibit high PL and good biocompatibility, rendering them useful for various biomedical and technological applications. For specific applications, the properties of CDs can be tailored by selecting appropriate precursor with specific functional groups. Waste materials such as tire soot, paper, and scraps have been successfully used in the production of CDs, leading to reduced pollution and lower production costs despite increasing concerns about sustainability [90,97]. Additionally, synthetic fibers can be produced using waste materials like household garbage and feces through the process of pyrolysis. This can result in the production of CDs that can be used in biomass-derived CDs production. There is a growing interest in reusing garbage to create valuable CDs, with a focus on converting agricultural effluents into useful products to achieve zero waste production and lessen environmental impact.

Hydrothermal carbonization is a cost-effective and environment-friendly method for synthesizing CDs at controlled high temperature in a closed autoclave [98]. The formation of N-CDs involves four processes: polymerization, aromatization, nucleation, and growth. Various biomass sources, including papaya juice [99], sweet pepper [100], banana (Musa acuminate) juice [101], garlic [102], citrus pectin hydrogel [103], husks of nuts [104], rice husk [93], sugarcane juice [105], pomegranate [106], apple [107], bagasse [108], and other green sources have been successfully used to prepare novel CDs using this hydrothermal method [109–113]. Table 1 provides a list of the bioresources used for preparing green CDs.

In addition to vegetables and fruits, CDs have also been prepared from a range of other biomass sources such as soy milk [114], bamboo leaves [115], grass [116], black tea [117], ginkgo leaves [11], and other plants and beverages. The size distribution of CDs prepared using these carbon sources is relatively uniform, and they disperse well in water. Moreover, researchers often utilize kitchen waste, including fruits and vegetable peels [118], fish scales [119], cauliflower leaves [120], and office waste paper [121], to produce high-quality CDs. Different leaf sources, such as ginkgo leaves [11], bamboo leaves [115], willow bark [122], aloe [123], and lignin biomass [124], can also be utilized to prepare various types of CDs using this simple approach.

In addition to improving the yield and narrowing the band gap of CDs, the performance of doped CDs can be changed by heteroatom doping. Researchers have used lignin biomass as a carbon source to prepare CDs with uniform size distributions, achieving a QY of 21 % [124,125]. They have also used cocoon silk [126] and gelatin [127] as carbon sources, resulting in QYs of 31.6 % and 38 %, respectively, using an economical and simple hydrothermal method. Additionally, green sources contain various vitamins and minerals. The hydrothermal process can incorporate elements such as P, N, and S from green sources through self-doping. Examples of green sources include egg white (Fig. 2a) [128], soybean milk [114], cucumber juice [129], watermelon [109], pipe tobacco [130], oatmeal [131], garlic [102], molasses [2], ginger [132], grass [116], yam [133], dried shrimp [134], and more.

Well dispersed CDs have been synthesized by using fruits and their wastes, such as Lu et al. [135] hydrothermally synthesized low-cost, water-soluble CDs using grapefruit peel. This method involves adding peels to water and then heating them in an autoclave at 200 °C for 3 h. Through centrifugation and drying of the solutions, a QY of 6.9 % was achieved with an average particle size of 2–4 nm. Using strawberry juice treated at 180 °C for 12 h, Huang et al. prepared nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles [136]. The synthesis method was simple, cost-effective, and eco-friendly, resulting in spherical particles with an average of 5.2 nm. CDs were produced using cabbage as a carbon source through a facile and green hydrothermal protocol [137]. After smashing the cabbage, it was autoclaved for 5 h at 140 °C. After cooling, the solution was filtered and centrifuged at 12,00 rpm for 15 min, yielding a brown solution. A purified solution of CDs was obtained using tubular dialysis membrane and water. The spherical particles obtained had a size ranging from 2 to 6 nm and a QY of 16.5 %. Similarly, Wang et al. [138] used hydrothermal carbonization of papaya to synthesize 2–6 nm green CDs, obtaining a QY of 18 % by heating papaya at 200 °C for 5 h, followed by filtering and extracting the products via dialysis for 72 h. However, no information was provided on the QY, making scaling up and determining efficacy not feasible. Atchudan et al. [139] described the hydrothermal production of nitrogen-doped CDs using unripe peaches and aqueous ammonia as a nitrogen source. The synthesized N-CDs had a size of approximately 8 nm, with low cytotoxicity, QY of 15 %, excellent biocompatibility, and good water dispersibility. It is worth noting that this method is not as environmentally-friendly due to the use of ammonia as a nitrogen source. Fig. 2b illustrates the hydrothermal synthesis of CDs using unripe peaches. Similarly, Arul and Sethuraman [140] utilized Phyllanthus emblica fruit as a carbon source for the synthesis of N-CDs (4.08 nm) in aqueous ammonia. By following similar procedures, CDs have been successfully prepared with various bioresources such as Boswellia ovalifoliolata bark (Fig. 2c) [141], salep flour [142], groundnuts [143], limeade [144], porphyra polysaccharides [145], and hylocereus undatus [146]. A green method of producing water-soluble CDs was developed using Saccharum officinarum juice as the carbon source. The S. officinarum juice was subjected to heating at 120 °C for 180 min in an autoclave with ethanol, resulting in a dark brown solution. The unreacted organic moieties were removed through centrifugation at 5000 rpm and washing with dichloromethane. As a result, 3 nm spherical particles were obtained [105]. Fig. 2d shows the hydrothermal treatment process for the synthesis of CDs from S. officinarum juice [116].

Plants, leaves, and grass were applied for the production of green CDs. Concomitantly, to produce spherical CDs with an average particle size of 5 nm, fresh aloe was subjected to hydrothermal treatment [123]. This involved heating a 50 mL solution of aloe with 5 g of aloe and 25 mL water in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 11 h. After cooling, the solution was filtered using a 0.22 mm diameter membrane, resulting in a yellow-colored solution. Any remaining organic matter was removed using dichloromethane. Fresh Tulsi leaves were then mixed with distilled water and heated in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for 4 h, leading to a dark brown color solution. The CDs were obtained by filtering and dialyzing the solution. The process is illustrated in Fig. 2f. A study conducted by A. Kumar and colleagues discussed a similar method for producing CDs using pseudo-stems of banana plants as the carbon source [147]. The procedure involved rinsing pseudo-stems with water to remove dust particles, mixing the juice with ethanol, and heating it a Teflon-lined autoclave for 2 h at 180 °C. After washing with dichloromethane, a dark brown solution was obtained, which was then centrifuged to obtain CDs. CDs were also synthesized using sweet potato as the carbon source through a similar hydrothermal treatment process [148]. Another method for producing CDs, which is easy, green, and cost-effective, was developed using rose-heart radish [149]. The chopped radish was heated in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 3 h. The solution was then distilled for 24 h using a dialysis membrane with a molecule weight cutoff of 500–1000.

The solvothermal technique is widely used as an effective technique for synthesizing CDs-based materials. It involves subjecting the material to high-pressure and temperature, which promotes the nucleation and growth of CDs. Common solvents like N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), dimethylformamide, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and benzene are utilized in this technique [194]. To being, a carbon precursor, such as citric acid, glucose, or any another organic compound, is dissolved in a non-aqueous solvent like ethanol. The solvothermal synthesis route offers various advantages, including simplicity, cost effectiveness, size control, and the ability to perform large-scale synthesis of CDs [195]. Compared to hydrothermal method, solvothermal synthesis actually performs much better on size control, narrow emission and high PL QYs.

In this process, precursor materials are often added to solvent and placed inside a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave reactor. This provides the high pressure and temperature needed for synthesizing CDs. To prevent the CDs from clumping together during the process, a stabilizing agent is also added to the solvent mixture. One of the advantages of this method is that the high temperature and pressure of the system ensures that the synthesized materials have a uniform size distribution. Additionally, the sustained temperature increases the reaction kinetics, leading to a higher production of reactive intermediate species. These intermediates are responsible for the dehydration, polymerization, and carbonization of the materials, resulting in the formation of CDs in a shorter period of time. Furthermore, the properties of the CDs can be adjusted according to the desired outcome by varying the reaction time.

Fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles (FNCPs) can be synthesized using strawberry juice by doping them with nitrogen at a concentration of 6.88 % nitrogen [136]. The process involves placing strawberry juice in an autoclave lined with Teflon and heating to 180 °C for 12 h. The large particles are then separated through centrifugation after filtering them out with a 0.22-μm membrane. The products are obtained after 72 h of drying under a vacuum (Fig. 3a), resulting in a high QY of 6.3 %. Selective quenching of FNCP by Hg2+ was observed in this study. For the synthesis of CDs, an autoclaved lined with polytetrafluoroethylene was used to heat citric acid and ethylenediamine in deionized water, producing a brown-black transparent product. After cooling to room temperature, the product was dialyzed to obtain CDs [196]. (Fig. 3b). A single-step solvothermal method using citric acid as a precursor and six alkyl amines as solvents was employed to prepare multicolor solid fluorescent CDs [197]. The presence of alkyl chain amine solvents affected the interactions between solid CDs, influencing their fluorescence properties and quantum efficiency.

Meng and colleagues have reported the solid-state synthesis of combined red, green, and blue bandgap fluorescent carbon quantum rings (R/G/B-SBF-QRs) with a QY ranging from 30 to 46 % [198]. Fig. 3d illustrates the solvothermal treatment of the reaction substrate at 120–160 °C for various reaction times using two precursors: p-phenyldinitrile containing cyano and phenylenedicarboxylic acid containing aldehyde groups. A simple solvent-washing procedure was then used to purify the product, resulting in the formation of different-sized CD rings by linking quantum bands of varying lengths.

These particles are high soluble in water and other polar solvents such as acetone, tetrahydrofuran, DMSO, dimethyl formamide (DMF), and ethanol. Due to their low toxicity and biocompatibility, these GQDs have great potential towards bioimaging applications. In previously reported method by Zhang et al., the reduced GO quantum dots (rGOQDs) synthesized using DMF through solvothermal method resulted in particles with green emission [199]. This fluorescent behavior of QDs could be attributed to the fact that GO consists of both sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms and functionalities that enhance the solubility of this material, resulting in fluorescent behavior of QDs. For the synthesis of GOQDs and nitrogen doping of this material, GO was initial reduced. This reduction of GO resulted in a higher QY of 24.62 % compared to GO (6.53 %). The increased fluorescent behavior of these particles clearly indicates the effect of nitrogen doping within them.

In microwave synthesis, organic substances are carbonized using microwave radiation to produce CDs. A shorter heating duration can improve the QY and quality of the CDs. However, microwave radiation is potentially harmful to humans, complicating its large-scale production application. Nevertheless, this method is efficient, effective, and time-saving, resulting in high efficiency and homogeneous heating, which is critical for the formation of high-quality CDs [200–203]. Various green CDs are synthesized using microwave radiation methods, as listed in Table 2 .

Amino acids are yet another green source recently reported for the production of CDs. Bajpai et al. [200] demonstrated a microwave reaction using milk protein casein as a carbon source. By heating the casein solution in the microwave, a semi-solid solution was obtained. The supernatant was obtained by centrifuging the residue and diluting it with distilled water. This method is environmentally-friendly as it requires fewer steps and avoids the use of harsh chemicals. To make it even more eco-friendly, waste biomass could be used instead of lab-grown biomass. The QY of these dots is high at 18.7 %. Another study used microwave treatment to prepare CDs by mixing bovine serum albumin with urea as a carbon source [204]. Fig. 4a illustrates the synthesis process for N/S-CDs. The CDs prepared had a QY of 14 % and a size distribution between 2.4 and 5 nm. Water-soluble 1–4 nm CDs were rapidly synthesized using the microwave method with flour as a carbon source [205]. The size of the CDs decreased as the concentration and temperature of the reactants decreased, with a QY of 5.4 %. Rose [203], eutrophic algae [206], and other natural materials have also been used in the microwave method for CD synthesis. The microwave method using rose as a carbon source was found to be effective and simple [203]. Fig. 4b, c and d show a schematic of the synthesis process using rose, denatured protein and algae, respectively.

The microwave method is commonly used to prepare CDs doped with heteroatoms. For example, in a study, silkworm chrysalises [207] were used as a precursor for preparing water-soluble N-doped CDs. These CDs had a diameter of 19 nm and a QY ranging from 40 to 45 %. In another study [208], N-CDs were prepared using natural lignocellulose as a carbon source in an ethanol-water co-solvent with an acid catalyst system. A one-step microwave method was used, resulting in N-CDs with an average diameter of 2.6 nm and a distribution ranging from 2 to 3 nm. Microwave radiation has been increasingly used in recent years but at a lower frequency compared to hydrothermal method. Feng and colleagues [203] also used microwave irradiation to synthesize CDs with a high QY of 13.45 % using rose petals as the carbon precursor. In a separate study, microwave oven was used to heat glycerol and phosphate solution for 14 min, resulting in the production of multicolor CD [209]. This synthesis method is easy, economical, and environmentally-friendly. It can also be performed by using other carbohydrates such as glucose, glycol, sucrose, etc. Additionally, Wang et al. synthesized N and S-co-doped CDs using citric acid as the carbon source and glutathione as the N and S sources in a microwave-assisted one-pot process. This resulted in high QY CDs with an average particle size of 1.8 nm. These CDs demonstrated good biocompatibility, water dispersibility, photostability, and low cytotoxicity [210]. Microwave methods have also been used to fabricate CDs from lotus roots [211], lignin [208], and gelatin [202].

Fluorescent CDs can be rapidly and economically synthesized using microwave energy [212]. A solution containing polyethylene glycol and sugar was heated in a microwave oven set at 500 W for 2–10 min, resulting in the formation of CDs. The size of the obtained CDs was 3 nm with a QY of 2.46 %. During the microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment of potato starch as a biomass precursor, the obtained CDs with a size of 3 nm showed a QY of 2.46 % (Fig. 6e) [213]. Carbon-doped CDs and nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) were also synthesized under the same conditions, with N-CDs showing a larger size of 4.5 nm and double the QY of undoped CDs. Both CDs and N-CDs exhibited flue fluorescence under UV light, excellent water solubility, and thermal stability, with N-CDs showing higher fluorescence power.

Another microwave-based method involved heating a solution of citric acid, L-cysteine, and NaOH in a microwave oven (750 W) for approximately 3 min, resulting in the synthesis of solid-state fluorescent multicolored CDs (Fig. 6f) [214]. Goose feathers were used as a carbon source to produce QDs with a size of 21.5 nm and a QY of 17.1 % emitting blue fluorescence [215]. Highly luminescent CDs with sizes 1–6 nm were also obtained by heating ethanol and tender coconut water in a microwave. The size of the CDs varied depending on the temperature at which they were synthesized, with CDs prepared 180 °C showing a QY of 2.8 % and emitting blue and green light under excitation at wavelengths of 390 and 450 nm, respectively [12]. Microwave-assisted synthesis of CDs has also been carried out using various biomass precursors such as Palm kernel shell and eggshell [216–218] as well as and bamboo bar [219], resulting in the formation of fluorescent CDs with different emission color and sizes. The CDs and CQDs synthesized in these studies exhibited high QY and fluorescent properties.

The synthesis method of green CDs involves pyrolysis, a well-established and simple process that carbonizes organic substances at high temperatures (300–800 °C) under inert atmospheric conditions, such as nitrogen. However, this method requires high temperatures and further passivation of the dots [229–231]. Table 3 provides details on the pyrolysis of various green precursors used in the CDs synthesis. By using pyrolysis, CDs with particle size ranging from 0.4 to 6 nm and QY ranging from 3 to 25 % can be achieved.

Various plant sources, including gynostemma [232], grass [233], rice husks [93], and leaf tissues [234], can be used in this method to prepare CDs. For example, CDs were prepared from biomass obtained from rice husks [93]. In one study, watermelon peels were carbonized in the air at 220 °C for 2 h. The carbonized product was then dispersed using a sonication process and centrifugation to obtain a solution of CDs. After dialyzing the solution with water, amorphous CDs were obtained with an average particle size of 2 nm and high water solubility, which exhibited spherical shapes [235]. The optimal temperature for the pyrolysis of plant leaves, including oriental leaves (16.4 % QY), lotus leaves (15.3 % QY), and pine needles (11.8 % QY), was found to be 350 °C. The pyrolysis was carried out without the use of any solvent, using a quartz boat under a nitrogen atmosphere in the temperature range of 250–400 °C. A schematic diagram in Fig. 5e illustrates the synthesis process using watermelon peels. Sweet red pepper, a low-cost carbon source, was used by Yin and colleagues to synthesize highly sensitive CDs with both down- and up-conversion emissions [100]. The fresh pepper suspended in water was heated in an autoclave lined with Teflon and ceramic plates for 5 h at 180 °C. The large particles were separated through centrifugation, and the filtrate was purified by dialysis with water to obtain purified CDs. This synthesis method also has the advantages of simplicity, high yield, and potential applications in environmental and biological assays.

The study by Sun et al. described the synthesis of sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (S-N-CDs) by carbonizing hair fibers with sulfuric acid [229]. The process involved adding H2SO4 to hair fibers, followed by sonication for 30 min, which caused a color change from brown to black precipitates. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 8 using NaOH after sonication with water. Filtration was then used to remove larger particles, resulting in a deep yellow solution that could be dialyzed to obtain S-N-CDs. The reaction at high temperatures led to the formation of S-N-CDs with higher S- content. The synthesized CDs were found to be stable, low in toxicity, biocompatible, and highly soluble. Fig. 5f provides a schematic representation of the hydrothermal carbonization of hair fibers to obtain CDs. Another synthesis method described by Zhang et al. [231] utilized marigolds as a carbon source for pyrolysis synthesis for CDs. The process involved compression and fermentation to form pellets, which were then pyrolyzed to obtain the final product. Nitrogen doping with ethylenediamine after reduction with H2SO4-HNO3 was also done. However, this method involved multiple steps and harsh chemicals, resulting in a less green process and a lower QY of 7.84 %. Further development is needed to make this method viable (Fig. 5b).

A simple, low-cost, and green pyrolysis method was employed to produce bio-based CDs from waste materials such as peanut skin [236], coffee grounds [237], litchi exocarp [238], waste frying oil [239], litchi seeds [240], peanut shell [241], watermelon peel [235], etc. For example, waste-soluble CDs were prepared using litchi seeds as carbon sources via simple pyrolysis [240]. The properties of the green CDs obtained by pyrolysis vary depending on the specific preparation methods used for peanut shells [241] and peanut skin [236]. Various other carbon precursors have also been utilized for CDs synthesis, including peanut shells, [242] crab shells [243], gum Arabic [244], and different plant leaves [234].

Chemical oxidation, also known as chemo-oxidation, is a method of oxidizing a substance by causing it to lose electrons after being exposed to an oxidizing agent, such as H2O2. This process introduces oxygen-containing functional groups, such as −COOH and − OH, which make the substance very hydrophilic and create tunable surface states for the dots. Chemical oxidation is a convenient method for rapid large-scale production as it does not require complicated equipment [246]. It also offers high selectivity; however, there are some disadvantages, including environmental pollution associated with the use of expensive oxidants and a low production capacity with intermittent production. Biomass is rarely used as a carbon source for preparing CDs using chemical oxidation.

One such method, known as Hummers' method, is used for the synthesis of CDs using graphite, KMnO4, NaNO3, and H2SO4. Based on this method, Liu et al., developed GO and GOQDs through a simple and rapid one-pot solvothermal method using various carbon sources such as carbon black and platanus leaves [274]. The particle size was controlled by adjusting the temperature and graphite to permanganate ratio (G:P) during the reaction. By increasing the temperature from 130 to 140 °C (for GO synthesis) to 150, 160, and 170 °C, GO, CDs (42.84 ± 0.51 nm), and GOQDs (2.4 nm) were successfully synthesized in addition to GO. This method is suitable for the production of large-scale GO and GOQDs by adjusting the temperature. Additionally, the size of GOQDs can be adjusted by varying the graphene to permanganate ratio (G:P). Furthermore, this method can be used for the synthesis of CDs using different carbon sources, including carbon black, SWCNTs, MWCNTs, as well as Platanus leaves [275]. Another method involves using activated carbon with nitric acid for 24 h, neutralizing with NaOH, and purifying the solution using water-immersed dialysis membranes and centrifugation. The synthesized CDs had graphitic structures with diameters of 3–4 nm [276]. Using the H2SO4/HNO3 (3:1) at 140 °C for 7.5 h, Soriano et al. discussed the top-down synthesis strategy of CDs using carbonaceous nanomaterials (MWCNTs, SWCNTs, and C60) [277]. CDs were precipitated using a mixture of acetone, ethanol, and water after chemical reaction and centrifugation. The CDs from MWCNTs were monodispersed and had a uniform diameter of around 3 nm. Table 4 lists the synthesis conditions and parameters of obtained CDs using the oxidation method.

Fig. 6a illustrates the use of chemical oxidation is to synthesize CDs from biomass-derived carbon [278]. In a study by Feng and Zhang, coke was used as a biomass and subjected to ultrasonic conditions for 30 min in a mixture of H2O2 and water to prepare CDs [279]. Intense stirring was then employed to heat the mixture at 40 °C for 3 h. The solution was filtered using a dialysis bag to remove larger impurities, resulting in CQDs with an average dimension of 6.5 nm and a QY of 9.2 %. Similarly, Peng et al. [280] chemically synthesized CDs through oxidation, starting with carbon nanoparticles formed by decomposing carbon material with nitric acid and treating with sulfuric acid to remove the water from a carbohydrate solution. PL was observed after combining amine-terminated compounds with the carbon nanoparticles, as shown in Fig. 6b.

Boruah et al. [281] have also successfully produced CDs using ultrasonic-assisted chemical oxidation from taro peels, garlic peels, and sugarcane bagasse waste materials. These CDs were highly water-soluble, exhibited strong blue fluorescence under UV light, and had an excellent QY of 4–27 %. Furthermore, photoluminescent CDs were prepared from three different activated carbon sources, where activated carbon was etched into CDs after nitric acid treatment and passivated with amine terminal compounds (Fig. 6c) [273]. In another study, CDs made from waste frying oil and concentrated H2SO4 were developed [239], displaying blue luminescence with a QY of 3.66 % and a size of 2.6 nm. Water-dispersible CDs with outstanding fluorescence characteristics were also synthesized using starch as a cost-effective carbon source [282]. Starch was carbonizede under pressure, then treated with an oxidant solution containing distilled water, HAc, and H2O2, followed by ultrasonication, refluxing, and filtration. The resulting solution was dialyzed using a dialysis bag, neutralized, and concentrated with NaOH before being vacuum dried to obtain a light-yellow powder of CDs, as shown in Fig. 6d.

A simple chemical process was used to produce CDs from carbohydrates. Concentrated sulfuric acid was added to dissolve carbohydrates (sucrose, glucose, and starch) under stirring, followed by adding water [280]. After centrifuging the solution, a black carbonaceous powder was collected and washed. A 50 mL nitric acid was added to the carbon powder and refluxed for 12 h, then neutralized and dialyzed to remove soluble impurities. Surface functionalization of the carbogenic nanoparticles was achieved using 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine TTDDA. The mixture of CDs and TTDDA was heat treated at 120 °C for 72 h and dialysis treatment was used for further purification. The resulting CDs had a crystalline structure with an average diameter of approximately 5 nm. CDs can also be generated from activated carbon (achieved from wood, coal, or coconut) through a simple and low-cost method. Dispersed activated carbon was sonicated in an aqueous solution of HNO3 for 10 min, then refluxed, cooled, and centrifuged. The mother liquor was neutralized by Na2CO3 and dialyzed to purify the solution. The CDs produced had an average particle size of 4.5 nm and were water-soluble, with remarkable photostability and biocompatibility [273].

In this process, biomass is dehydrated, polymerized, and carbonized using ultrasound waves. Huang et al. [287] provided an example of using cigarette ash in an ultrasonication synthesis. They used a cell disruptor to ultrasonically heat cigarette ash and PEG-SH for 30 min. The remaining raw materials were removed by centrifuging and dialyzing the supernatant liquor for 3 days with distilled water and alcohol. Rotary evaporation was then used to isolate the CDs. This method is particularly interesting due to its automatic PEG functionalization, eliminating the need for further reaction. The dots displayed notable optical stability under long-term UV light exposure, had a uniform size of 4 nm, and exhibited excellent water dispersibility and photostability. A significant advantage of this method is that the precursor material is easily obtained through recycled tobacco ash, making it suitable for scaling up. However, the efficiency of this reaction cannot be determined due to the lack of yield information.

The mixture of PEG, urea, and citric acid are the main sources for the ultrasonic processing production of CDs. Wu et al. [288] used an ultrasonic method to synthesize amine-decorated CDs from graphite rods. The resulting CDs due to their carbon core and small size, are believed to possess lubricating properties [289]. Thambiraj et al. [283] employed a low-temperature synthesis approach to produce CDs. They first combusted sugarcane bagasse pulp at 60 °C and then extracted and subjected it to ultrasonication. This methodology enabled the researchers to convert food waste and agricultural waste into CDs [290,291]. Specifically, waste animal skin was used to produce microwave-assisted CDs. The process involved heating pre-roasted skin at 750 W for 25 min. Ultrasonication was then performed during the later stages of the process to form CDs [292].

To address issues related to yield, Park et al. [291] developed a large-scale synthesis method for green, water-soluble CDs using waste food. They mixed 100 kg of food waste with 500 L of 10 % ethanol for synthesis. The mixture underwent ultrasonic treatment for 45 min, followed by centrifugation at 4500 rpm for 5 min to remove large, agglomerated particles. The used a 0.22 μm membrane to filter the CDs twice, eliminating any remaining large particles. The final yield was nearly 0.12 % from biomass, resulting in 120 g of CDs from 100 kg of food waste. These CDs demonstrated excellent solubility in water and had a size range of 2–4 nm.

Compared to traditional quantum dots (QDs), the isolation and purification of CDs present a greater challenge due to the presence of numerous hydrophilic surface groups and a lower carbon core density. When synthesizing CDs, there is often a significant amount of reaction precursors and by-products, necessitating a combined purification process involving filtration/centrifugation for the removal of larger particles, as well as dialysis for the elimination of small molecules/ions [162,212]. Achieving a uniform size distribution is crucial for obtaining high-quality CDs, and further advancement in post-synthesis separation technology is vital. Dialysis, ultrafiltration, electrophoresis, column chromatography, and their combinations have proven effective in obtaining purified and precisely regulated CDs of various sizes.

A method that combines centrifugation and filtration is used to extract naturally occurring or processed CDs, such as those found in cooking or brewing. This method is low-cost and does not require complex treatment procedures, but it lacks control over certain properties [34,293,294]. Wang et al. [293] reported extracting CDs from beer for imaging and drug delivery. To remove gas from the beer, 100 mL was stirred and concentrated using rotary evaporation. After filtering out large particles, chromatography was used to purify the concentrate, resulting in an average particle size of 2.5 nm. These highly soluble CDs achieved a QY of approximately 7.39 %. However, no information was provided about the production yield, so it remains unclear whether scaling up will be efficient.

The usage of fried food residue were reported for extracting green CDs [294]. In this process, fried food waste is mixed with water and stirred to form a colloidal solution, which is then separated from the water using filter paper with a pore size of 0.22 μm. Through centrifugation at 10,000 rpm, the CDs were separated from the supernatant. This method does not involve any synthesis and has the advantage of not requiring heating or harsh chemicals, making it inexpensive and simple to use. However, it cannot be use to tune the properties of the CDs.

For this purpose, researchers have also employed various other simple and creative methods to synthesize green CDs. For example, Phadke et al. [295] used neem gum in a biogenic method to synthesize CDs. The CDs obtained were stable and measured at 5–10 nm under different temperature and pH conditions. Romano et al. [296] synthesized N- and S co-doped CDs by photochemically reacting an alkaline vegetable extract with PEG. The size of the CDs decreased with increased UV irradiation time. Jayanthi and colleagues prepared CDs from carrots by refluxing them in a trisodium phosphate solution [297].

In summary to the above discussion, it could be concluded that the hydrothermal method is the most commonly used technique for synthesizing green CDs because it is cost-effective, ease of use, and eco-friendliness. However, this process takes several hours to complete. The phase purity and structural uniformity of CDs, in addition to size distribution, has been extensively studied. This is concerning because impurities such as amorphous carbon, oligomers, and carbon particles are inevitably present. To meet the requirements for high-quality CDs in various applications, it is crucial to develop new separation and purification methods. Additionally, factors like size, morphology, crystallinity, and surface specificity should be taken into account [298] as they play a significant role in adjusting optical performance and facilitating applications in biological medicine and devices. Drawing inspiration from traditional QDs, exploring methods like gradient ultracentrifugation could be a promising direction worth pursuing [299,300]. In Table 5 , both types of synthesis methods are compared in terms of their advantages and disadvantages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification strategies for the green synthesized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs obtained from biomass exhibit a wide range of physicochemical properties, which are influenced by various factors such as functional groups, surface defects, shape, size, and edge configuration [301,302]. Generally, functional modifications can be divided into four categories based on whether they are synthetic or post-synthetic. These categories include (1) size and shape control, (2) element doping, (3) surface modification, and (4) composites development.

Numerous studies have shown that the size of CDs significantly influences their characteristics of CDs [15]. Researchers have found that different CD sizes affect the distribution of sp2 structures in the carbon nucleus, impacting the electronic energy level of the atom [303]. Additionally, CD PL varies with size [304], indicating that the size of CDs affect their PL performance. There is an inverse relationship between CD size and nucleation speed. Small particles have a relatively uniform size distribution due to rapid nucleation, which is influenced by the surface state of raw materials and their solubility [305]. As particle size decreases, the PL effect of CDs with similar surface states improves, leading to an increase in PL QY [6,306]. Currently, researchers are controlling CDs sizes between 2 and 10 nm to achieve high PL characteristics. The selection of carbon source and solvent is critical in controlling the size of bio-mass derived CDs, considering various practical preparation methods [307]. Furthermore, it has been discovered that ionic liquids regulate structure and size of CDs, further affecting their performance characteristics. Once nucleation clusters have reach a certain size, they remain stable with increased reaction time [308]. Ionic liquids with low surface energy promote rapid nucleation and formation of small particles, resulting in CDs modified by ionic liquids ranging in size from 0.6 to 1.6 nm [309].

The shape of CDs can vary depending on the temperature and density of the carbon clusters. They can exhibit triangular, hexagonal, trapezoidal, mushroom, or parallelogram shapes. Several CDs also have different edge defects, such as zigzag and armchair edges (Fig. 7a). CDs with zigzag edges have a lower band gap (0.14 eV) compared to those with dominant armchair edges (0.38 eV). The presence of localized states in the conduction band contributes to the smaller band gap of zigzag edges [310,311]. Conversely, CDs with armchair edges have an extended band gap, resulting in blue-shifted PL emission. The complex structure and uncertain cutting direction of source materials (biomass) lead to CDs with a combination of zigzag and armchair edges. A study based on experimental data or density functional theory (DFT) calculations is needed to understand the PL mechanism dependent on CD edges.

CDs that have not been modified typically have low QY and fewer surface functionalities, which limits their potential applications. However, the luminescence properties of CDs can be effectively adjusted by doping them with elements that have energy levels controlling Fermi energy [312]. Consequently, element-doped CDs have attracted significant research interest [313,314]. Doping is usually done with metals or heteroatoms, depending on the intended application. Metal doping involves the use of metal ions like Ni2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Gd3+, which can modify the chemical and physical properties as well as the charge density of CDs [315,316]. However, this method is limited by the toxicity associated with high concentrations of metal ions. As a result, researchers are now exploring heteroatom doping as a promising area of research. By doping CDs with oxygen, sulfur, and nitrogen atoms, the electronic structure of CDs is altered, and the performance depends on the interactions between the orbitals. This alteration affects both the sp3 hybridization electron region in the carbon core and the sp2 hybridization electron orbital region on the surface. Through heteroatom doping, CDs can achieve a narrower bandgap, wider absorption, and higher QY. Consequently, the energy gap between the π O, S, and N π-orbitals and the n orbitals of C shrinks (Fig. 7e). Notably, compared to undoped biomass-derived CDs, self-doped biomass bovine serum albumin [317], chitosan [318], and tar [319] exhibit higher QYs of 44 %, 31.8 %, and 26.1 %, respectively. A hydrothermal method of N-doping CDs (9.8–17 % of nitrogen) using ethylenediamine and citric acid has been successfully developed, producing a large quantity of N-doped CDs with an industrial-scale production rate (PY of 58 %) and high-quality (QY of 80 %), with similar performance to semiconductor QDs and commercial organic dyes [196]. Other successful heteroatom dopants include Si [320], F [321], S [322], P [323], and B [324]. Doping with S expands the emissivity of traps and increases the excitation band gap energy of CDs, leading to an enhanced QY [325,326]. Through hydrothermal treatment and adding a platinum catalyst, Wang et al. produced controllable S-doped GQDs from durian. A lattice substitution of the S atom produced a sp2 heterocyclic and thiophene structure with a high level of reforming efficiency.

Co-doping, which involves the simultaneous doping of multiple heteroatoms, can lead to novel CDs with unique electronic properties [178,327]. For instance, treating hair fibers with H2SO4 produced CDs with co-doped with S and N atoms that had different binding configurations for S and N, including −C − Sox, thiophene-S, pyrrolic-N, and pyridinic-N (Fig. 7d). Microwave-assisted heating of MWCNTs in an ionic liquid medium also yielded N, S, and F-co-doped GQDs, with an average size of 2 nm with 85 % yield [328]. However, when it comes to low-cost CDs doping, it is advisable to use biomass materials rich in heteroatoms [329].

Coordination and electrostatic interactions link the functional groups to the surface of CDs, which help reduce surface defects and prevent non-radiation recombination [330]. This connection between the functional groups and the CD surface strongly influences the fluorescence emission of CDs and is affected by surface oxidation and functional groups [331]. The presence of different surface passivation can explain the variations in QY of CDs under the same excitation wavelength [6]. Surface passivation plays a crucial role in improving the PL stability and QY of CDs [332]. To prepare CDs that emit PL, it is essential to select suitable biomass precursors. However, bare CDs surfaces contain numerous defects that decrease PL intensity, stability, and QY. Surface passivation, by blocking traps and defects on the CD surface, enhances electron and hole recombination efficiency, leading to 10–30 % increase in QYs for CDs [333,334]. Effective surface passivators also enhance the hydrophilicity of CDs and often serve as functionalizing agents by forming carbonyl groups, amino groups, and carboxyl groups on the CD surfaces.

The adverse effects of contaminants are countered by attaching organic polymers to the edges of CDs which form a thin barrier layer [335,336]. Some common surface passivators include PEG [23,337], amines such as urea, PEI [338], ethylenediamine, ammonia, etc. Various methods, such as covalent bonding [339], sol-gel methods [340], and coordination [341], have been developed to passivate CDs surfaces. CDs, which have abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, can form covalent bonds on their surfaces. For example, CDs prepared from cadaverine and glycine exhibited a QY of 30.6 % through covalent bonding (Fig. 7b) [342]. Covalent bonds can also cross-link multiple dots in PEG1500-passivated CDs, further stabilizing the CD structure [343]. Coordination is another effective method for functionalizing CD surfaces. Many CDs have oxygen-containing groups at their surfaces, such as carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups. These oxygen-containing groups can coordinate well with Eu3+ ions, as the ions have an affinity for oxygen donors. The coordination of Eu3+ ions with the oxygen-containing groups on CDs transfers charge and causes quenching of the CD PL, acting as a bridge between neighboring oxygen-containing groups [341].

Recent research efforts have focused on developing composite materials with inorganic nanoparticles such as Cu2O [344], Fe2O3 [345], TiO2 [346], Ag3PO4 [347], and ZnO [348]. These nanohybrids have shown great potential as efficient photocatalysts or magneto-optical bioimaging agents by combining the magnetic, optical, and mechanical properties of CDs and oxide cores [24,349]. Zhang et al. demonstrated a simple hydrothermal method for fabricating Fe2O3/CDs composite in one step, which exhibited higher photocatalytic activity compared to pure Fe2O3 in the 550–800 nm wavelength range [345]. There are two potential modification mechanisms to consider: CDs act as electron reservoirs, preventing recombination of electron-hole pairs, and their up-conversion photoluminescent properties allow them to absorb longer wavelength light and emit shorter wavelength light, exciting metal centers to form electron-hole pairs. To improve photocatalytic activity, Pan et al. synthesized a series of TiO2/CQDs composites by electrospinning CDs solution at various concentrations. The CDs in the composite showed excellent up-conversion emissions and optimal photocatalytic activity when irradiated with visible light (400–780 nm) [350].

An innovative approach to designing and fabricating advanced catalysts based on carbon composite materials show promise for cleaner energy and a cleaner environment. CD composites and other carbon-based materials prevent material aggregation, increase surface area, expand light utilization, and enhance light absorption capabilities. Wang et al. developed composites with varying loadings of functionalized carbon by heating a mixed solution of CDs and graphene at 100 °C for 24 h. Drying the graphene sheets prevented convolution and created a porous structure. CDs/graphene composites exhibited a significant increase in specific surface area up to 1256 m2 g−1 compared to pure graphene. When used as supercapacitor electrodes, these composites demonstrated an exceptionally high specific capacitance of 324.6 F at 0.3 A current density (Fig. 7c) [351]. Li et al. reported that thermally treating GQDs and biomass-activated carbon electrodes at 90 °C for 3.5 h produced an excellent candidate for use as a lithium-ion battery electrode. The presence of GQDs significantly reduced the electrode charge transfer resistance by 5-folds and increased the lithium diffusion coefficient by 175-folds [352].

Recent advancements in CDs synthesis have shown progress in producing monodisperse and narrow-bandwidth emitting CDs [298,356]. For example, triangular CDs with a half-peak width of only 20 nm have been developed. A promising strategy for achieving monodisperse CDs is total organic synthesis, which involve the preparation of CDs with large conjugated molecular cores (containing over 100 carbon atoms) and alkyl surfaces. This approach allows for atomic-level dimensional uniformity and structural reliability [357,358].

Bottom-up synthesis methods are simple, eco-benign, and cost-effective as compared to the top-down approaches. Moreover, these methods provide control over size and shape of CDs, therefore bottom-up techniques are frequently used for CDs synthesis. For the synthesis of CDs, both PL behavior and QY are considered as crucial factors and can be controlled by varying the particle size and shape. Additionally, both PL and QY of CDs are highly influenced by experimental parameters including temperature, reaction media, selection of dopants, type of carbon precursor, and the content of carbon source in the reaction medium [359]. A variety of CDs can be prepared by using different carbon precursors and synthetic routes [76]. Among various other synthesis methods, hydrothermal and microwave-assisted synthesis methods are commonly used for utilization of valuable biomass as these synthetic routes are easy to operate and provide control over scalability. Moreover, these methods are considered as solvent-free processes that can be used over a range of highly abundant biomass precursors without any limitations of the moisture content of raw materials. The hydrothermal method not only generates CDs with high photoluminescent QY but also provides an autologous high pressure which increases the digestion efficiency of organic material and maintains the temperature and volume of the system for longer time period [79].

In summary, there is still a lack of systematic re-evaluation and rational thinking of the CDs process. For instance, the utilization of low-cost biomass involves the risk of unintentionally introducing trace amounts of non-metallic or metallic doping, resulting in significant changes in their structure and properties [360–362]. In considering the historical development of traditional QDs, it is important to acknowledge that future large-scale production will likely differ greatly from laboratory systems. Therefore, more attention should be dedicated to the quality and performance of CDs [298,363,364], rather than solely focusing on the cost of ingredients.

The formation of green synthesized CDs involves several complex chemical reactions, including decomposition, dehydration, condensation, polymerization, nucleation, and aromatization (Fig. 8 ) [353,365–367]]. However, characterizing the intermediate products in each reaction step is challenging, resulting in an ambiguous mechanism for CD formation from natural feedstocks [149]. The formation of precursors is influenced by various factors such as surface functional groups, carbon chain lengths, and numbers of carbon atoms, leading to various structures and physicochemical properties of green synthesized CDs [368]. The interaction between natural precursors during hydrothermal carbonization is also unclear and requires urgent clarification. Top-down methods, which involve physical, chemical, or electrochemical methods for breaking down bulk carbon materials, such as carbon fibers [369], carbohydrates [280], activated carbon [273], carbon soot [370], etc., play a key role in CD formation [371]. Scientists often use waste carbon materials such as tire soot [372], candle soot [373], natural gas soot [370], etc. in green synthesis. Strong oxidants like H2SO4 and HNO3 are used to break down these large carbon materials into nanosized CDs. However, top-down synthetic methods are less common in green synthesis due to limited raw materials.

In the hydrothermal conversion of biomass, hydrogen and glycosidic bonds are sequentially broken. During hydrolysis, cellulose and hemicellulose form oligomeric-reducing sugars (primarily levoglucosan) composed of C/H/O elements. These reduced sugars are then converted into soluble small molecules like 1,2,4-benzene-triol, hydroxy acetaldehyde, and hydroxymethyl furfural derivatives through dehydration and pyran ring opening. These intermediates, along with polysaccharides), undergo dehydration and condensation to form polymers. Finally, cellulose and hemicellulose are converted into CDs, which have abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the side chains and furfural structures and polyaromatics at their cores (Fig. 9 ) [374].

In bottom-up synthesis, the organic molecules undergo pyrolysis or carbonization. This synthesis method involves a series of steps including condensing, polymerizing, carbonizing, and passivating organic molecules. Various chain intermediates can be produced using small organic molecules through condensation reactions like aldol condensation [101,375], amidation [196,376,377], radical reaction [378,379], and Schiff base condensation [380–383]. Covalent, non-covalent, or other interactions further polymerize or aggregate the intermediates into polymer-like CDs using certain polymer carbon clusters [384]. These CDs undergo carbonization, especially at high temperatures, to form the carbon core [385–387]. Additionally, adding surface passivation agents improves the luminescence efficiency of residual precursors on CD surfaces. Bottom-up synthesis is typically used to obtain greener CDs by carbonizing organic molecules in biomass materials at high temperatures since biomass materials mainly consist of small organic molecules. On the other hand, a top-down approach involves cutting CDs from small-sized carbon wastes such as tire soot and candle soot. In the study by Liu and colleagues [388], N and S co-doped CDs were formed through five stages: decomposition, self-polymerization, aggregation, carbonization, and surface passivation. The process involved using L-cysteine aggregation as a seed to polymerize it into small dots, which then formed N and S-CDs through continuous carbonization of crystalline carbon nuclei at high temperatures.
