Another study by Zhang and colleagues [389] demonstrated the formation of Gd-CQD in three stages: nucleation, growth, and aggregation. Ethylenediamine and citrate acid were polymerized to create tiny carbon nuclei, and a Gd3+ chelate was formed on the surface of neighboring CDs, leading to the formation of Gd-CD hybrid material. Subsequent aggregation and carbonization process resulted in the visible appearance of an insoluble substance after a 4 h hydrothermal reaction.

Currently, the research on the synthesis mechanism of CDs is significantly lagging behind that of traditional QDs. Precise investigations on how precursor reactivity is regulated and how the nucleation and growth stages are separated are largely lacking. This lack of understanding of the synthesis mechanism has become a major obstacle in the field, hindering further development in terms of optical properties and applications. One reason for this lag is relatively short history of CDs development. Additionally, carbonization reaction itself is a more complex free radical reaction compared to the coordination or electrostatic interaction-based reversible adsorption of ligands seen in traditional QDs synthesis. This complexity makes controlling the reaction more difficult.

Moreover, the harsh conditions required for the synthesis of CDs, such as high temperature and pressure, contribute to the complexity of the multi-step organic reactions and the formation of multiple intermediates. To overcome these challenges, it is important to explore new and more suitable CD precursors and develop new reaction pathways. CDs have evolved into an interdisciplinary field involving nanoscience, materials science, and organic chemistry, and thus, the synthesis mechanism research should consider the collaborative intersection of these fields [48,390]. Promising directions for exploration could include finely controlled electrochemical etching and surface functionalization of graphite, total organic synthesis, and controlled conversion of single molecular weight polymers. One particularly important aspect for understanding the growth process, size, and surface control, and property customization of CDs is the total synthesis methodology of organic conjugated macromolecules [162,391,392]. This method could provide valuable insights for further advancements in the green synthesis of CDs.

CDs possess both molecular PL and phosphorescence properties [62,304,393]. Additionally, these CDs have been found to possess tunable luminescence across the entire UV–visible-near-infrared spectral range. Furthermore, quasi-bandgap emission originating from the carbon structure has been observed [393–395]. However, there is an ongoing debate regarding the quantum size effects [304,396].

Although different types of CDs possess distinct chemical structures, they share similar absorption characteristics, i.e., a strong absorption band in the UV region (230–320 nm) accompanies by a tail that extends into the visible region. For example, MWCNTs can be electrochemically oxidized to generate CDs, which exhibit a broad absorption around 270 nm with a narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 50 nm [397]. A recent study showed that surface modification can enhance the absorbance of CDs [51]. When CDs are passivated by TTDDA, the absorption wavelength is increased within the 350 to 550 nm range, resulting in higher emission intensity in this region [280]. Additionally, distinct absorbance peak centered at 262 nm is also observed in CDs treated with PEG-200 and NaOH [398].

There is a interplay between QY and PL intensity in CDs, which is primarily influenced by doping [399] and surface passivation [6]. Furthermore, the PL of CDs is affected by pH level, particle size, and the characteristics of surface moieties [400]. By effectively adjusting doping, surface passivation, and PL emission wavelength, a higher QY can be achieved [313,401].

The emission performance of CDs is characterized by its luminescence QY (Fig. 10 ). Fluorescence is the type of light emitted when absorbed light is converted into emitted light [402]. A high QY fluorescence can be achieved even at low concentrations, making high-quality CDs valuable tools in various fields such as biomedicine, optics, and energy [403]. Previous reports indicate that many bare CDs have an average QY below 10 % [402]. However, the QY of CDs can be improved by doping their surfaces. For example, Zhou et al. [235] found that CDs made from watermelon peels as carbon precursors had a fluorescence QY of 7.1 %. Similarly, Parvin and Mandal [404] utilized agarose is the hydrothermal synthesis of N-doped CDs, while Liao et al. [171] demonstrated that N-doped CDs had a significantly higher QY of 28.46 % compared to non-doped CDs at 5.31 %. Evaluating the effectiveness of CDs as luminous materials requires an understanding of their fluorescent QY, which is crucial for applications such as cell imaging. The higher the QY value, the better the optical performance of CDs.

QY for ZnS-doped CDs was 78 %, while it was 70 % for TiO2-doped CDs [405]. Besides doping inorganic semiconductors, nonmetals such as boron, [406] phosphorus, [407–409] fluorine [410], sulfur [411,412], and nitrogen [413] can also enhance the PL QY of CDs. Passivation of CDs increases PL intensity, with higher amounts of passivating agent resulting in greater intensity [62]. However, once a certain intensity of PL is reached, further increase is not observed. Currently, organic molecules and polymers are the primary passivating agents. PL intensities were significantly increased following passivation with 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC). QY of CD increased from 18.83 % to 41.10 % with an increase in EDC concentration from 2.5 mM to 25 mM [414]. The properties of CDs can be modified through adjustment of synthesis conditions, such as adding oxidizing or reducing reagents, which alter the surface composition and subsequently modulate the photochemical properties. This adjustment of synthesis conditions allows for optimization of alkali concentration during the synthesis process [415].

CDs have fascinating optical property called PL, which means they emit photon when exposed to external light. The exact mechanism of PL in CDs is not fully understood [400], but researchers have identified two main mechanisms: the conjugated p-domain band gap transitions and the CD surface defect states [416]. Some studies suggest that the redshift of CD emission can be attributed to quantum size effects, meaning that the particle size influences their PL behavior [48]. However, the absence of quantum size effects in most CDs suggest that their chemical composition and structure play a larger role in their PL properties [395]. The surface state of CDs, which refers to functional groups on their surface, also affects their PL [417,418]. The presence of surface energy traps and different types of energy traps created by surface functional groups contribute to the PL of CDs [419]. The p-electron structure of electron-hole pairs in the sp2 state contributes to the surface state defect-derived PL of CDs. It is primarily surface-state luminescence that is responsible for the luminescence of doped CDs. As a result, doping CDs can greatly increase the PL quantum yield [312,420]. Some CDs exhibit up-conversion PL [62,421], where they absorb two or more photons with longer wavelengths and emit light with shorter wavelength. These CDs with up-conversion PL properties are particularly useful for in vivo imaging as they can penetrate tissues more effectively and have longer excitation wavelengths.

However, the size and composition distribution of CDs prepared by different research groups vary significantly, and there is still no universally recognized benchmark synthesis method. This lack of standardization increases the difficulty of studying the quantum confinement effect of CDs. Many CDs exhibit PL in the blue region that is dependent on the excitation wavelength. This could be due to multiple trap states or carrier recombination paths related to CDs of different sizes and surface groups, but the exact mechanism is currently unclear. A thorough understanding of the excitation wavelength-dependent PL mechanism through experimental research may help to elucidate the luminescence mechanism and defect states of CDs. In addition to this, CDs have shown other novel optical properties such as up conversion PL, solid-state phosphorescence, laser emission, and aggregation-induced luminescence [214,422–424]. However, the origins and mechanisms of these novel phenomena have not been fully understood. Importantly, key parameters of CDs such as PL QY and FWHM still lag behind those of traditional QDs, which could hinder further development in device and biological applications. Recently, triangular CDs with adjustable emission colors and a half-peak width of 20–30 nm have been reported, suggesting a potential breakthrough in PL performance [425].

At present, the PL mechanism of CDs is still controversial, and research on the excited state mechanism remains limited [426–428]. Some work has used time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy to measure the PL lifetime of CDs and provided initial explanations for their luminescence mechanisms. Proposed mechanisms so far include the quantum confinement effect of conjugated sp2-domains, defect states, molecular states, cross-linking enhanced emission effects, and core-surface electronic coupling. However, ultrafast photophysical studies, especially femtosecond transient absorption, are still relatively rare in the field of CDs compared to the extensive research conducted on traditional QDs. The systematic ultrafast spectroscopy research in the field of CDs is still at early stage, and there is an urgent need to improve synthesis methods in order to obtain reliable and high-quality properties. The properties of CDs are closely tied to their preparation methods, and currently, there is a lack of uncontroversial standard samples that can be widely accepted. This lack of standardization is one of the biggest obstacles in the development of the CDs field. Therefore, cheap biomass-derived CDs face a natural disadvantage, and it is necessary to use biomass-derived small molecule precursors with clear molecular structures to control the luminescent properties of CDs.

CDs can also be used to create optoelectronic devices through solution processing methods. However, current research primarily focuses on PL and electroluminescence devices [429,430]. Initially, CDs were utilized as inexpensive phosphors that were coated onto the surface of light-emitting diodes. These diodes typically include UV or blue GaN LED chips and phosphors made from CD composite materials, which acted as a spectral conversion coating. In more significant applications, fluorescent CDs can be used as a light-emitting layer sandwiched between an organic hole transport layer and an organic or inorganic electron transport layer [298,393,431,432]. By choosing CDs as dopants for organic LEDs, it is possible to achieve high quantum efficiencies comparable to traditional QDs-based LEDs. Nonetheless, LEDs based on solely CDs tend to have broad electroluminescent peaks and FWHM values that often exceed 80 nm. As a result, their use in high-performance devices is limited [298].

CDs offer advantages such as high emission spectra, high energy density, and narrow emission bandwidth, making them ideal for use in liquid crystal displays and illumination devices. In addition, CDs hold promise as a potential replacement for traditional LED raw materials due to their similar luminescence characteristics to CDs, as well as their environmental friendliness and low toxicity [403,431]. The WLED prepared by Miao and his group [390] has CIE coordinates of (0.33, 0.34), which are close to pure white light. Researchers have experimented with different reaction conditions to synthesize multi-colored CDs by controlling the thermal pyrolysis of urea and CA with PLQYs of 7.4 %, 29.8 %, and 38.6 %, respectively for Red, Green, and Blue LEDs, demonstrated to emit strong light using CD/epoxy composite materials.

Zishan Yan and colleagues used a simple one-step solvothermal reaction between trimellitic acid and o-phenylenediamine to create white-light-emitting carbon dots (WCDs). The synthesized WCDs were characterized using PL spectra and column chromatography, revealing four types of CDs: blue (BCD), green (GCD), yellow (YCD), and red, which when combined, produce white light. Experimental data and theoretical simulations showed that the PL and emission spectrum redshift are influenced by carbon core and surface states. LEDs were produced by synthesizing the CDs, with WCDs yielding a low color rendering index (CRI) of 82 when used as phosphors for white LEDs. Fig. 11a,b illustrates the preparation of WCNDs and WLEDs [433]. Additionally, it was demonstrated that a rare-earth element (Tb3+) can be functionalized on the surface to create white LEDs. CDs with high QY were synthesized from Plumeria leaves using an enzymatic hydrothermal process, exhibiting electroluminescent behavior independent of stimulation. By combining a UV-LED chip with functionalized CDs, a device was created that produces cold white light with a correlated color temperature of 4995 K and the Coefficient Internationale de L'Eclairage coordinates of (0.33, 0.34) [434]. Furthermore, ethanol was used as the carbon source for the production of white LEDs (Fig. 11c). Wang et al. demonstrated that synthetic CDs emit blue, cyan, and yellow wavelengths similar to WCDs emission centers through column chromatography separation. Despite having similar graphic structures, the surface of the samples varied in levels of oxidation and carbonization, leading to a red shift in their emission peaks due to higher carbonization in their polymer structure. Multicolored and white LEDs were successfully built using the synthesized CDs [435]. Yuan et al. successfully prepared multicolored light-emitting CDs in blue, yellow, green, and red colors using phenylenediamine as a precursor [431]. Monochromatic CQEDs had luminescence levels as high as 136 cd m−2, and a warm-color WLED was constructed with a PLQY of 53 % using blue, green, and purple LED chips [436], achieving a CIE standard, correlated color temperature is 3875 K and CIE coordinates of (0.39, 0.39), with a CRI of 97 and a LE of 31.3 lm W−1 (Fig. 11d-f). The researchers also prepared CDs with triangular structures exhibiting pure-color luminescence with PLQY rates if 54–72 %, and CQEDs with LBs up to 4762 cd m−2 [437].

One of the challenges in CDs as light-emitting components is the light reabsorption effect or non-radiative decay processes that occur when CDs are aggregated in solid state matrixes. To overcome this obstacle and achieve high PL QY, it is crucial to find an appropriate matrix where CDs can disperse homogeneously. Additionally, a solid-state luminophores show promise for future lighting and display technology. Researchers have used a layer-by-layer approach to deposit CDs onto UV-LED chips, and then mixed them with transparent polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) in a chloroform solution. This resulted in a bathochromic shift light when more CDs were deposited on the outer layer, as the outer layer absorbed short wavelength light from the inner layer. By increasing the CQD/PMMA layer thickness, the emission color of LEDs could be easily controlled between blue and red [438]. A study conducted by He et al. [439] involved preparing fluorescent materials with double emission peaks at 420 nm and 635 nm (bCDs/rCDs/MAs) by doping blue-emitting and red-emitting CDs into mesoporous alumina. By combining 395 nm ED chips with bCDs/rCDs/MAs and adjusting their mass ratio, white light with CIE coordinates (0.336, 0.258) was emitted. Another study involved preparing a CD/Zr-MOF composite with PL QY of 37 % by combining CDs with Zr-MOFs [440]. A WLED with a high CRI of 82 and color coordinates of 0.31 and 0.34 was fabricated by coating InGaN-based LED chips with the CD/Zr-MOF composite.

One advantage of CDs for large-scale application in devices is that they do not contain toxic elements. In the development of next-generation displays with a wide color range, QDs that have high quantum efficiency and narrow emission bands will be important. However, CDs still lag behind traditional QDs in terms of precise control of structure and performance. There is a need for a better understanding of key factors defects, recombination, and scintillation, that determine device efficiency in the field of CDs.

Traditional QDs are important in studying photocatalytic mechanisms and designing photocatalysts. On the other hand, CDs have unique advantages in enhancing light absorption, charge transfer, and catalysis promotion. Currently, CDs have a wider range of applications in photocatalysis and electrocatalysis compared to traditional semiconductor QDs [304,441,442]. Additionally, CDs serve as a connection between traditional carbon materials and organic chemistry, making them valuable for understanding industrial carbon-based catalysts. However, CD-based catalysts are still in their early stages of development and there is much progress to be made in controlling their structure, studying their ultrafast spectroscopy mechanism, and characterizing their in-situ catalytic mechanism. In these aspects, CDs still fall behind traditional QDs.

Many researchers are showing interest in using photocatalytic technology as a way to produce energy and degrade pollutants in an eco-friendly manner. It has been discovered that CDs can absorb a wide range of light, from deep ultraviolet to near-infrared. The ability of CDs to transport electrons is due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer, and they can serve as excellent donors and acceptors of electrons. Because of this, CDs are seen as a promising new type of photocatalytic material [443]. For example, Han and coworkers [444] found that nitrogen-doped CDs could easily be incorporated into hybrids made of amorphous and mesoporous materials, and demonstrated high photocatalytic activity when using visible light to degrade nitrobenzene derivatives.

Yu et al. [445] developed a TiO2/CDs hybrid through hydrothermal treatment, with varying ratios from 0.5 to 2.5 wt%, for photocatalytic H2 evolution. CDs acted as both photosensitizers and electron reservoirs to enhance the separation of electron-hole pairs and sensitize TiO2 for hydrogen evolution. Li et al. [446] synthesized a Cu2O/CDs composite that showed enhanced near-infrared photocatalytic activity. The enhanced efficacy was attributed to the up-conversion of CDs and the greater light-reflecting ability of Cu2O, resulting in a 90 % degradation efficiency for organic dye. Shen et al. used a simple refluxing method to enhance the photocatalytic performance of degrading RhB dye using a CD/p-InVO4 nanocomposite [447]. The optimal nanocomposite decomposed RhB photocatalytically at a rate 1.25 times faster than commercial catalysts and twice that of p-InVO4, with CDs fascinating the transfer of photogenerated electrons to p-InVO4 and the porous structure of nanocomposite absorbing more RhB. Wang et al. [448] dispersed CDs in graphene aerogels to create a metal-free catalyst. The unique porous structure of graphene aerogels allows for the separation and exposure of photogenerated carriers, which are generated by CDs. The composite of CDs and graphene aerogels exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activity, with the optimal removing 91.3 % of Cr (VI) after 40 min of UV and Vis irradiation, compared to pristine CDs.

Efficiently converting CO2 into fuel through photocatalytic technology is an effective way to achieve sustainable energy development and meet global energy demands. To achieve this, it is critical to develop high-capacity photocatalysts with high charge separation efficiency and CO2 adsorption. CO2 is responsible for 65 % of all global greenhouse gases, making it a significant contributor to global warming [449,450]. By reducing CO2 through photocatalysis, we can lower atmospheric CO2 levels while also generating valuable fuel and chemicals, such as acetic acid, formic acid, methanol, and methane [347]. However, there are several challenges to overcome in the process of photocatalytic CO2 reduction, both from a thermodynamic and kinetic perspective. Thermodynamically, the reduction of CO2 requires electrons with higher reduction potentials. Kinetically, the transfer of electrons and protons to CO2 must occur sequentially or simultaneously, making the reaction complex and multi-step [451].

In a recent study, Kong et al. investigated the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 using CDs and ultrathin Bi2WO6 [452]. The found that the CD/Bi2WO6 nanocomposite exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance when exposed to pristine visible light. Compared to pristine Bi2WO6 platelets and bare Bi2WO6, the nanocomposite showed a significant enhancement in CH4 production, with a total yield of 7.19 μmol/g catalyst. This improvement can be attributed to the nanostructures and large exposure of the [001] facets of Bi2WO6, which enable efficient harvesting of NIR light. Additionally, the CDs help prevent recombination of charge carriers generated by photoinduced photons.

Solar energy can be used to reduce CO2 emissions by using composites made from TiO2-modified carbon materials. Researchers have found that TiO2 nanotubes can effectively reduce CO2, and this capability can be enhanced by using CDs derived from biomass. By using a hydrothermal method with waste orange peel as a precursor, monodisperse CDs with a diameter of 2–5 nm can be efficiently produced along with modified TiO2 nanotubes for CO2 reduction [453]. When compared to TiO2 nanotubes alone, the photocatalytic performance of CD-modified TiO2 nanotubes is considerably improved under irradiation. The yield for CH4 and CO generation is 2.5- and 2.4-times larger, respectively. Additionally, biomass-derived CD-modified TiO2 nanotubes have a stable CO2 reduction ability. In another study conducted by Ong et al., they used CDs-hybridized protonated g-C3N4 to perform photocatalytic CO2 reduction under visible and simulated solar light [454]. Among the various nanocomposite materials tested, the CDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposite with 3 wt% CDs showed the highest photocatalytic activity. After 10 h of visible light irradiation, 59 μmol/g catalyst of CO and 29 μmol/g catalyst of CH4 were produced. The photocatalytic activity of CDs/g-C3N4 remained relatively constant even after four consecutive photo-reaction cycles. This improved performance can be attributed to the effective transfer of photoinduced electrons from the conduction band of g-C3N4 to the conduction band of CDs, which reduces electron-hole recombination and enhances photocatalytic activity.

CDs have numerous advantages that make them ideal choice for use as photocatalyst in water splitting [455–457]. These advantages include their non-toxic nature, stability, abundance, and low cost [458]. As the research for green and renewable energy continues, photocatalytic hydrogen production has emerged as a highly exploited field. This process utilizes sunlight as a zero‑carbon emission green source to split water into H2 and O2 through photocatalysis. CDs, when combined with other photocatalysts, offer a range of surface morphologies, a large surface area, and tuned band energy, allowing them to be used as co-catalysts [459–461]. Water splitting using visible light can be achieved in two ways. The first approach involves a single one-step excitation mechanism using only one visible light photocatalyst [462]. However, this process has a positive Gibbs free energy (∆G0), indicating an unfavorable reaction from a thermodynamic perspective.

This reaction can only be driven by a semiconductor photocatalyst with a band gap energy suitable for accepting photons with energy equal to or greater than its band energy. When photons interact with the photocatalyst, they stimulate the movement of electrons from the valence band to the conduction band, resulting in the formation of holes. The photogenerated electrons can reduce H+ into H2 if the bottom of the conduction band has a more negative potential than the H+/H2 potential (0 V vs. SHE). On the other hand, if the potential of the valence band is more positive than the potential for H2O/O2 (1.23 V vs. SHE), the holes can oxidize water into oxygen [463]. To maximize the visible light capture and improve solar-to‑hydrogen conversion, it is crucial to keep the band as narrow as possible [464–466]. However, a narrow band gap can diminish the driving force needed to overcome the redox energy barrier.

In another approach known as Z-scheme mechanism, two photocatalysts are utilized in a two-step excitation process [467,468]. To create a symbolic representation of the Z-scheme, a shuttle redox mediator, an O2 evolution photocatalyst, and an H2 evolution photocatalyst are required. In this mechanism, the photogenerated electrons in the conduction band reduce H+ to form H2, while the photogenerated holes in the valence band oxidize the reductive mediator. The photogenerated electrons then activate the conduction band of the O2 evolution photocatalyst, leading to the oxidation of the as-reduced mediator. Conversely, the photogenerated holes activate the valence band, oxidizing water to produce O2. The Z-scheme requires photocatalysts with valence band maximums below H2O/O2 potentials and conduction band minimums above H+/H2 potentials. This enables the use of various narrow band gap materials as photocatalysts. Under visible light, photocatalytic water splitting is driven by the electrons and holes generated by the narrow band gap materials. The performance of photocatalysts in both mechanisms is governed by key steps such as separating photogenerated electrons and holes, surface catalytic reactions, and rapid charge migration to surface sites without trapping or recombination. Factors such as surface area, surface morphology, crystal structure, crystallinity, and crystal size influence all of these steps. Photocatalysts containing CDs, in addition to semiconductors, have strong UV–vis absorption, indicating their effectiveness in absorbing photons in this range. The electron-hole pairs generated at the conduction band of CDs can easily transfer to acceptor semiconducting material and are less prone to recombination. CDs are believed to enhance the photocatalytic activity of the composite by preventing the recombination of electron-hole pairs and generating charge carriers (electron and hole) [469].

Using CDs and semiconductor composites to is an effective method to combat environmental pollution caused by organic pollutants. In recent years, there has been increasing interest in using CDs combined with semiconductors to enhance the degradation of organic pollutants through photocatalysis. This is particularly important due to the emergence of environmental issues caused by continuous emissions from vehicles and industries, including volatile organic compounds. The use of CD-semiconductor composites shows great promise for remediation and treatment of air pollution.

A study by Hu et al. developed hetero-structured CD-TiO2 photocatalysts and demonstrated their ability to degrade acetaldehyde under visible light [470]. They achieved a photodegradation efficiency of 99 % when using 3 wt% CDs/TiO2 under fluorescent light, compared to only 46 % efficiency when using pristine TiO2. While pristine TiO2 showed no photocatalytic activity under visible light, 3 wt% CDs/TiO2 exhibited a 30 % removal rate. The good removal of volatile organic compounds by the CD composite material under visible light can be attributed to three factors. First, CDs can capture visible light through their up-conversion PL properties. Secondly, the intrinsic functional groups and graphite-like electronic structure of CDs enhance the adsorption of organic compounds. Thirdly, photogenerated carrier electrons can be stored in CDs to inhibit recombination and generate •O2 − .

Photocatalysis technologies also have great potential for removing soluble organic pollutants from water. A research team performed a hydrothermal preparation of hybrid nanoflower-type ZnO/N, S-CDs [471]. The degradation rates of ciprofloxacin under simulated light and natural light exposures were 92.9 % and 85.8 %, after 20 min and 50 min, respectively. During a 120-min photodegradation experiment, the efficiency of antibiotics remained above 60 %. The presence of •OH and •O2− were found to play a crucial role in this reaction.

In another study by Han et al., [472] it was discovered that peroxymonosulfate degraded by 25.4 % methylene blue degraded by 31.7 % when both were exposed to visible light. However, when CDs were added simultaneously with peroxymonosulfate under dark conditions, only 19.6 % of methylene blue was degraded. This suggest that CDs themselves do not have a catalytic effect and cannot directly initiate reactive oxygen species generation from peroxymonosulfate. Semiconductor catalysts, on the other hand, convert light energy more efficiently due to their quantum size, which facilitates electron-hole separation. With 420 nm visible light, electrons and holes are produced depending on the band gap of the catalyst. These electrons are induced by light to migrate from the semiconductor catalyst to CDs, resulting in a separation of electrons from holes.

Dyes and other pollutants are disposed of into rivers, lakes, and oceans, causing pollution. The textile, paper, and plastic industries are the main contributors of these pollutants [473,474]. CDs play a significant role in environmental remediation by enabling photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants. Dye degradation can be achieved through direct or indirect mechanisms [474]. In the indirect process, redox reactions occur in the conduction and valence bands, generating reactive species such as holes, electrons, H2O2, O2–, and OH.

Guo and colleagues employed a facile impregnation-thermal method to prepare CDs/g-C3N4/ZnO composites for the photodegradation of TC [475]. The highest photocatalytic activity was observed with 30 wt% g-C3N4/ZnO in 4 mL of CD solution, resulting in 100 % degradation of TC within 30 min. This highlights the importance of CDs in improving the response to visible light and enhancing the separation of electrons and holes. Similar finding were reported by Hong et al., who found that samples with levels below 0.5 wt% CDs/g-C3N4 yielded the best results compared to samples with higher CD levels [476].

A nanocomposite made from organic soybeans, N-doped ZnO/CDs (N–ZnO/CD), was studied as a photocatalyst for the degradation of methylene blue. The results showed that 82.4 % of methylene blue degradation occured with the photocatalyst N–ZnO/CD nanocomposite, 60.3 % with N–ZnO nanocomposite, and 94.8 % with bare CDs. This indicates that nanocomposites are more effective at decomposing dyes compared to traditional materials [477]. Typically, CDs are used to degrade organic pollutants via photocatalysis. The photocatalyst was dispersed in Rhodamine B (RhB) solution (10 mg L−1) and stirred for 30 min in the dark before being exposed for 200 min to ionizing radiation by a Xenon lamp [478]. UV–Vis spectrophotometers were used to determine whether RhB degradation had occurred every 20 min (Fig. 12a). In the presence of a Xenon lamp, CDs degraded almost 100 % of RhB within 120 min. As the number of CDs increased, the photocatalytic efficiency increased.

Furthermore, researchers [479] discovered that alkali-assisted electrochemical method could be used to prepare to prepare CDs with up-conversion PL, which were highly efficient in driving the photocatalytic oxidation of benzyl alcohol into benzaldehyde with over 92 % conversion and 100 % selectivity (Fig. 12b). The same procedure was followed to produce CDs electrochemically using graphite and ZnIn2S4 (ZIS) [480], nanosheet composites as well as ZnFe2O4 (ZFO) [481], The two were found to be extremely effective at degrading tetracycline, removing 83 % and 79.5 %, respectively, after 120 min of exposure to visible light. The unique structure of the composites contributed to the enhanced responses to more visible light spectrums and longer charge separations, according to the researchers. ZIS promotes tetracycline degradation by generating hydroxyl and sulfate-free radicals because of the magnetic properties of the ZFO microstructure, photocatalyst recovery was increased.

There is considerable interest in CDs because they can also be used as electron mediators. By using a facile hydrothermal process, Huang and colleagues produced CDs/ZnFe2O4 composite photocatalysts that demonstrated roughly eight times higher transient photocurrents than the original catalyst [482]. Comparing CDs/ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles with pristine ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles, CDs/ZnFe2O4 demonstrated improved photocatalytic activities and high selectivity for nitrate generation. Photocatalytic degradation of CDs/ZnFe2O4 samples was facilitated by CDs acting as electron reservoirs and transporters, respectively (Fig. 12c). In another study, a composite of metal-free CDs/g-C3N4 was found to significantly improve the degradation of diclofenac compared to g-C3N4 alone [483]. CDs were not dramatically affected by the modification, but the optical properties of the composite were dramatically altered so that it could be applied to the photocatalytic degradation of diclofenac (Fig. 12d). A CD loaded with pure g-C3N4 performed 15 times better than one loaded with g-C3N4 when the kinetics of diclofenac degradation was optimized. Overall, these studies demonstrate the potential of nanocomposites and CDs in improving the efficiency of photocatalytic processes for degrading organic pollutants.

Di et al. used a facile hydrothermal approach to prepare Bi2WO6/CDs nanocomposite for the degradation of bisphenol A (BPA), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl), ciprofloxacin, and RhB dyes [484]. The highest photocatalytic activity for RhB degradation was observed in a hybrid material containing 2 wt% CDs and Bi2WO6, which degraded 64 % and 87 % of CIP within 20 and 120 min, respectively. Bi2WO6 demonstrated significantly improved efficiency in photo-catalyzing organic pollutants when CDs were present in all four cases.

Yu et al. used a hydrothermal and electrochemical method to prepare CD nanosheets and 2D TiO2 nanosheets [485,486]. The degradation efficiency of CDs in solution increased from 27.2 % to 95.4 % after irradiation with visible light. Nanocomposites with the highly reactive [001] facets of the TiO2 nanosheets exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activity due to their unique UCPL behavior.

Photoelectrochemical cells generate electricity by using n-type semiconductors as photoanodes and counter electrodes. Essentially, they produce hydrogen on the surface of the electrode in a non-spontaneous manner [445,487]. The electrons are transferred from the external circuit to the counter electrode, which generates H2. The holes in the photoanode connect to an atoning agent [445]. Currently, CuO, Si, TiO2, and ZnO are used as CD-based semiconductors in hybrid photoanodes. Using CDs with TiO2 has been found to enhance the efficiency of TiO2 photoanodes, increasing the current by five times in the visible range and doubling it in the UV range. A composite of NiFe/CD/WO3 developed by Cao and colleagues [488] for the PEC application exhibited an IPCE value of 38.6 % at 405 nm, which is four times greater than that observed with the WO3 photoanode. Compared to the pure WO3 photoanodes, the composite is three times more efficient, achieving an applied bias photon-to-current efficiency of 0.16 %. Furthermore, a photocurrent of 1.43 mA cm−2 was observed at 1.23, surpassing reversible hydrogen electrodes.

CDs have a higher light absorption rate with an increasing mass [489]. They act as the best sensitizer for the generation of PEC H2 by effectively activating TiO2 nanocrystalline. Liu et al. [490] explained that CD/TiO2 produces a photocurrent of 1.2 mA cm−2 at 0 V, four times greater than pristine TiO2. Impedance spectroscopy conducted by Bian and colleagues [491] revealed charges exchanged between electrode and electrolyte, while a study by Sundheep et al. [492] demonstrated a good conductivity of CDs. Anson-Casaos and colleagues [493] used an AUTO LAB PGSTAT302 N to create a PEC setup with three-electrode cells equipped with quartz windows (Fig. 13a-f). Graphite rods were used as the counter electrode, and a power of 150 W emitted by LOT-Oriel GmbH's Xe bend light was utilized. The intensity of the light, equivalent to sunlight, was 300 mW cm−2. The photocurrent density of Cu2S nanowires decorated with CDs at 0 V vs. NHE was reported to be four times greater than that of nanowires in their pristine state (Fig. 13i-n) [494].

Wang and colleagues [495] synthesized a biosensor by combining Mo2C nanotubes with CDs to identify double microRNAs. They achieved efficient sensing through energy transfer between gold NPs and CDs using the PEC system. The biosensor, which included Mo2C nanotube and CDs, demonstrated high photosensitivity, environmental friendliness, and biocompatibility. This allowed for exceptional electron transfer capacity and increased the PEC response of CDs, enabling the differentiation of various miRNAs at low level of 0.15–0.12 fM. Ma et al. [496] reported that a Ag-CBO/NGQD photocathode with co-adjusted Ag ions showed improved PEC action. The light-gathering efficiency was improved with NGQD loading, Ag particle doping, and 1D structure, resulting in more evenly distributed and mobile photo-generated carriers. The photocurrent density at the NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs photocathode under AM 1.5 G illumination was 1.8, 2.3, and 5.9 under with 0.3 V vs. RHE, which was 8.8-fold higher than other configurations (Fig. 13g and h). In addition to their impressive efficiency, their investigation methods are designed with minimal planning effort.

The study conducted by Zeng et al. [497] focused on the use of GQDs doped with nitrogen and decorated with ZnO to investigate the bifurcation of water. Carrier density measurements were taken for ZnO, undoped GQDs/ZnO, and NGQD/ZnO NWs, resulting in values of 4.5 × 1018, 1.02 × 1019, and 1.61 × 1019 cm−3, respectively. Another study by Zhang et al. [498] employed photoelectrocatalysis electrode containing CDs. The results showed that the current over time of the electrode reached a stable resistance after only 30 s. Furthermore, compared to TiO2, CDs exhibited a six-fold higher photocurrent. Decay time for CQD/TiO2 (1.75 s) was more than twice that of TiO2 (0.89 s), indicating that with higher decay values, electrons translocation becomes more efficient and recombination rate decrease. This supports the notion that CDs can enhance TiO2 performance by acting as charge carriers. Under visible light illumination, the photocurrent feedback displayed cycles patters of on/off. In the absence of light, both TiO2 and CDs/TiO2 showed poor feedback, but CDs/TiO2 still exhibited higher feedback than TiO2. Notably, the maximum feedback value can be achieved for CD/TiO2 (001) under illumination. The introduction of CDs into (001) TiO2 resulted in slight improvement in the visible range, as indicated by the incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) for CDs and TiO2 (001) under UV light (below 430 nm). The improvement proved useful for charge partitioning between CDs and TiO2 (001).

The development of CDs catalysts focuses on being a representative model for the intersection of graphene, organic chemistry, and biochemistry, rather than simply being highly efficient catalysts. By using CDs with crystalline carbon cores and functionalized organic surfaces as model catalysts, researchers have gained a better understanding of industrial catalysts, especially carbon-based supports. This understanding was achieved through studying the surface chemistry and catalytic behavior of carbon particles [499–502]. Therefore, it is important to optimize the synthesis of CDs, adjust the type and number of surface functional groups, improve their light harvesting ability, optimize and adjust the storage and transfer capabilities of electrons, and gain a deeper understanding of the structure-activity relationship in the CD catalytic system. In this field, advanced mechanistic understanding of molecules and interface design should be key consideration.

Because of their excellent electrical conductivity and high stability, CDs catalysts have emerged as potential alternatives to traditional noble metal catalysts. Additionally, doping heteroatoms such as nitrogen and phosphorus can further enhance their electrocatalytic performance. By combining CDs with porous materials, the electrocatalytic performance can be further improved while also preventing the aggregation of CDs. Among the various heteroatom-doped CDs, P/N-CDs and B/N-CDs demonstrate outstanding catalytic properties compare to Pt/C, for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) [503].

In a study conducted by Qiu et al., [504] an all‑carbon hybrid electrocatalyst was reported, consisting of N-doped CQDs decorated on graphene (NCDs/G). The electrocatalytic activity of NCDs/G was compared to Pt/carbon black (Pt/C) electrodes in 0.1 M KOH solution using cyclic voltammetry. While the N-CDs/G electrode showed no significant change, the Pt/C commercial catalyst exhibited a strong response. This new electrocatalyst demonstrated comparable activity, methanol tolerance, and durability to Pt-based electrocatalysts for ORR. Jiang et al. successfully synthesized a Ultra-thin shelled Cu2-xS/MoS2 quantum dots catalyst for electrocatalytic nitrogen reduction [505]. Compared to nonmetals or previously reported metal catalysts for HRR, Ru@CDs exhibited significantly high electrocatalytic efficiency. Furthermore, this novel catalyst demonstrated superior durability to Pt/C catalyst under alkaline conditions, as proven by polarization curves and durability measurements after 10,000 cycles in 1 M KOH. In another study by Zhang et al., [506] a non-noble metal electrocatalyst consisting of CDs/MoPs exhibited effective catalysis for HER. Over a 24-h period, the composite showed negligible current loss, high durability, a small Tafel slope (56 mV), and low onset potential (−0.08 V). These studies further emphasize the potential of CDs-based catalysts as alternative to traditional noble metal catalyst for various electrochemical reactions.

Due to increasing scientific and technological advancements, global energy consumption has risen. Consequently, it is crucial to prioritize the development of renewable energy sources that are both clean and efficient, while remaining affordable. One promising material in the field of energy is CDs, which have shown potential in significantly reducing costs and improving performance for solar cells, supercapacitors, and lithium-ion batteries (LIBs).

Supercapacitors stand out as the most optimal energy storage devices due to their high energy density, fast charge/discharge rate, and long lifespan [507]. By utilizing porous materials like carbonaceous compounds and porous metallic compounds, it is possible to enhance capacitance by storing more charge carriers, owing to the increased surface area. Furthermore, CDs possess abundant functional groups on their surfaces, which can serve as additional active sites [508].

In one study, Strauss et al. [509] used CDs to prepare porous graphene. The CDs were initially prepared by microwave-assisted thermolysis of urea and CA, followed by annealing. The CDs were then reduced into CDs/CO2 plasma using infrared laser, which was then converted into 3D-turbostratic-graphene. The cyclic voltammogram of the 3D-turbostratic graphene electrode showed a pseudo rectangular shape in the electrochemical window of 1, 5, and 10 V s−1. Galvanostatic charge-discharge curves at various current densities from 4 to 100 A g−1 demonstrated good capacitive behavior. For the supercapacitor electrode, a layer of electrodeposited NiCo2S4 was added onto biomass-derived CDs and graphene composite films (CDGFs) [510]. At a current density of 0.5 A g−1, the ternary composite electrode exhibited a capacitance of 1348 F g−1. The design of the electrode also showed outstanding cycling stability and remarkable mechanical flexibility, with 80.4 % of its capacitance retained after 10,000 charging/discharging cycles.

Another study by Zhao et al. [511] involved the use of CD nanosheets decorated with rGO in supercapacitors. The rGO/CDs-based electrode showed excellent electrochemical performance, with the highest specific capacitance of 308 F g−1 at 0.55 A g−1. The cycling stability of the rGO/CDs electrode was over 92 % after 20,000 cycles. CDs have promising electrochemical characteristics, which make them suitable for high-performance supercapacitors, such as CD/CF (carbon fiber) [512], CD/graphene [120], and CD/CNT [513], composites. By preventing the aggregation of added materials like graphene, CNTs, and carbon fiber, more active sites can be created with the dispersed CDs and different materials.

Due to their high capacity, large voltage, long cycling life, and environmental safety, LIBs are being widely used as rechargeable storage devices and their market is growing rapidly. The three most significant components of LIBs are the cathode, the anode, and the electrolyte. One way to improve the performance of electrode materials is by increasing their conductivity, which can be achieved through the use of CDs. CDs enable electrode materials to have more active surface sites, increasing their surface area and wettability. They also help in regulating the volume changes of the electrode during charging and discharging. Li et al. [514] fabricated LITO/N and S-GQDs using a microwave irradiation approach. The insertion of heteroatom-doped GQDs greatly accelerated charge transfer and enhanced the lithium storage capacity. The LTO/N/S-GQDs anode exhibited a discharge capacity of 126.5 at 10 °C and 254.2 at 0.1 °C and a capacity retention of 97 % at 2 °C after 2000 cycles, demonstrating remarkable performance [514].

Zhu et al. [515] proposed a triaxial nanowire array of CuO/Cu/GQD as the anode for LIBs. The use of GQDs on the nanowire arrays significantly enhanced surface conductivity and structure stability. The resulting battery electrodes exhibited exceptional long-term cyclability of over 1000 cycles, and performed well at high-speed transmission. Jing et al. [516] obtained similar results by coating Mn3O4 particles with CDs. The LIB exhibited high-performance large cycle stability, with a specific capacity of 934 mAh g−1. Under a high current density of 100 mA, this nanocomposite maintained 99 % capacity compared to the individual samples. Prasath et al. [517] used a CDs nanocomposite as an anode material for LIBs based on bismuth oxide (Bi2O3). The Bi2O3/CDs coin cell exhibited a discharge capacity of 1500 mAh g−1 at 2 °C, and the capacity remained almost 100 % for 2500 cycles. Zhao et al. [518] synthesized GQD-functionalized CNTs as anode materials for LIBs. The hybrid GQD-wrapped CNTs exhibited a specific capacity of 700 mAh g−1 after 100 cycles at a current density of 100 mA g−1. The rate capability was also high, with a capacity of 483 mAh g−1 at 1 A g−1 after 350 cycles.

Photovoltaics (PV) are crucial for renewable energy sources today and in the future. In the next 25 years, PV technologies are expected to be the most effective way of meeting global energy demand. Expanding the availability of these technologies will help us achieve sustainability and renewable energy. Research has improved the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of thin film PV technologies through the development of new light-harvesting materials, electrocatalysts, and device modification engineering [519–523]. Although our understanding of these technologies is improving, there are still challenges preventing their widespread use, such as low conductivity of electrodes, poor electron collection abilities, and low photon absorption by sensitizers. To overcome these challenges, using carbonaceous catalysts is crucial.

CDs have shown promise in optoelectronic and energy storage applications due to their ability to accept and donate electrons, excite electrons, and absorb light. They can also be used as sensitizers in solar panel to capture and harness sunlight [524]. CDs have been combined with dye/QD sensitizers for PV applications because of their photophysical and optoelectronic properties [524–527]. For example, Liu and colleagues [528] created photoelectric devices using CDs and N719 dyes, which exhibited excellent electron extraction properties. TiO2/NCD/N719/CD/S-CD cascaded together had power conversion efficiencies of 0.208 % and transferred electrons efficiently. Similarly, Briscoe et al. [526] synthesized a solid-state nanostructured solar cell using ZnO nanorods and three different types of CDs as sensitizers. The researchers hydrothermally carbonized glucose, chitosan, and chitin to produce CDs with different fluorescent QYs of 1.4 %, 13.4 %, and 11.6 %, respectively. Coating ZnO with these CDs resulted in improved efficiency, with chitin-CDs and chitosan-CDs combination showing the highest efficiency of 0.077 %.

Dou et al. [529] utilized the hydrothermal method with red algae to obtain CDs. These CDs had an average size of 4.87 nm and a QY of 3.12 %. They exhibited good water solubility and a high QY. CDs can be used for light harvesting in mesoscopic solar cells. In comparison to solar cells without CDs, N719-customised solar cells showed a 1 % increase in maximum power conversion efficiency. Meng et al. [530] pioneered CD solar cells derived from biomass, where PEC-excited CDs were combined with LPP storage to create all-weather solar cells. Liu et al. [528] developed energy-graded designs of heteroatom-doped CDs to construct defect-free absorbers for DSSCs. Biomass was converted into CDs using hydrothermal methods, with co-doping of sulfur and nitrogen. These DSSCs performed well as PV devices due to broadened solar absorption and enhanced electron extraction. To improve the PV performance of QDSSCs, CDs were used as sensitizers, similar to DSSCs. Erbium-doped CDs (ECDs) were synthesized for the first time using a hydrothermal approach and used as sensitizers in QDSSCs [531]. CdS-CDs and E-CDs were successfully adsorbed and reacted on a TiO2 photoanode, resulting in different layers of QDs and E-CDs. The photoanode with CdS/Er-CDs/TiO2 efficiently absorbed and transported electrons, leading to a higher efficiency (0.43 %) than CdS/TiO2. Guo et al. [525] investigated the effect of different PV parameters on CDs prepared from pollen (B-CDs), glucose (GCDs), and CA (C-CDs). Improved efficiencies were achieved with CQDs-based sensitizers due to their better charge kinetics and broad solar absorption spectrum.

In recent years, there have a significant amount of research focusing on carbon-based quantum dots and related nanomaterials. While most studies concentrated on the PL properties of these nanomaterials, which are commonly associated with CDs, there is growing interest in the photoinduced redox processes that occur in these nanomaterials in various energy conversion applications. CDs have unique characteristics, such as the ability to absorb a wide spectrum of sunlight, separate charge upon photoexcitation, and generate electrons and holes that can drive chemical reactions, particularly in the production of H2 from water-splitting and CO2 reduction. These exceptional features distinguish CDs from other nanomaterials.

This review presents an evaluation of the green synthesis routes and photochemical performance of CDs from various biomass-derived precursors. The synthesized green CDs have various functional groups, eliminating the need for surface-passivating agents. Waste materials, natural foodstuffs, and biomass derivatives have been widely explored for CDs synthesis, and there is potential for using inexpensive and underutilized sources in in the future. However, the mechanism for CDs synthesis is yet to be fully explored. Among various synthesis methods of synthesizing CDs by using biomass, both electrochemical and hydrothermal methods are advantageous for their ability to easily control the morphology and composition of the CDs. This control allows for the achievement of uniform particle sizes and a large surface. Various factors, such as morphology, size, surface functionalities, and heteroatom doping, can impact the chemical and electronic structures of CDs. Doping not only improves their physiochemical properties but also increases their electrical conductivity. While most studies have centered around the PL properties of these nanomaterials, which are often considered as characteristic of CDs, there is growing interest in the unique qualities across various energy storage and conversion schemes, such as the ability to absorb a wide spectrum of sunlight, separate charge upon photoexcitation, and generate electrons and holes that can drive chemical reactions.

Despite the promising results of green synthesized CDs, there is still room for further development, both in the synthesis process and in the application of energy conversion.
•
CDs offers numerous advanced properties that can be utilized to improve existing device design and expand their potential applications in the future. Despite extensive research, the development of reliable and efficient prototypes for PEC applications from CDs still requires significant progress.
•
The primary goal of research on new materials is to develop smarter materials for state-of-the-art applications, which can eventually be produced on an industrial scale. However, scaled-up synthesis remains a significant challenge. In addition, other limitations associated with CDs must be addressed to make them commercially viable. Most CDs research has been conducted in laboratories, and it is crucial to investigate whether large-scale production yields the same characteristics. Therefore, the production of CDs using green precursors should be thoroughly investigated.
•
Control of CD surface functionalization during synthesis is challenging, where biomass-derived CDs have multiple functional groups affecting their properties and selectivity. It is crucial for CDs to possess the appropriate selectivity for each application.
•
To maintain vibrant CD research, consistency in synthesis, characterization, purification, and nomenclature is crucial due to reproducibility and protocol vagueness issues.
•
Specific applications of CDs, such as catalytic and fluorescent sensing, hard polymeric applications, and nanomedicine, have unique requirements. Effective utilization requires considering these application-specific needs. We ardently hope for the establishment of a CD centric sustainable system in the near future (Fig. 14 ).
•
Green CDs as not extensively used as photocatalysts compared to those derived from chemical processes. Exploring the use of green CDs in photocatalysis is necessary, and choosing the right precursors can enhance their photocatalytic activity. Additionally, CDs with a longer emission wavelength should be developed to expand the visible range. Investigating time-resolved spectroscopy and nuclear magnetic resonance can contribute to understanding how CDs catalyze degradation.
